options: Visualizing high-dimensional data in 2D or 3D
Imputing missing data point values
Training deep neural network models
Selecting the most relevant features
explanation: t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) reduces high-dimensional data to 2D or 3D for visualization while preserving local structure.

text: In association rules, what does 'support' measure?
options: The conditional confidence level of the rule
The overall predictive accuracy of the rule
The proportion of transactions containing an itemset
The total number of features in the dataset
explanation: Support measures how frequently an itemset appears in the dataset, calculated as the proportion of transactions containing that itemset.

text: What is the difference between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering?
options: Neither method uses a hierarchy
Agglomerative is bottom-up; divisive is top-down
Agglomerative is top-down; divisive is bottom-up
The two methods are completely identical
explanation: Agglomerative clustering starts with individual points and merges clusters bottom-up, while divisive starts with one cluster and splits top-down.

text: What is the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)?
options: A tree-based decision boundary model for supervised classification prediction
A probabilistic model assuming data comes from a mixture of Gaussian distributions
A linear regression method for predicting continuous numerical target values
A filter-based feature selection technique using statistical hypothesis tests
explanation: GMM models data as arising from a mixture of multiple Gaussian distributions, using the EM algorithm to estimate parameters, providing soft cluster assignments.

text: How does spectral clustering work?
options: Uses deep neural networks to learn cluster assignment mappings
Uses decision tree ensembles to group data into discrete clusters
Uses eigenvalues of a similarity matrix to reduce dimensionality before clustering
Applies K-Means clustering directly without any preprocessing transformation
explanation: Spectral clustering constructs a similarity graph, computes its Laplacian's eigenvectors, and clusters in the reduced eigenspace, handling non-convex shapes.

text: What is the UMAP algorithm?
options: A dimensionality reduction technique based on manifold learning and topology
A gradient-based numerical regression training technique
A supervised multi-class classification prediction method
An unsupervised density-based spatial clustering algorithm
explanation: UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is a manifold learning technique for dimensionality reduction that often preserves more global structure than t-SNE.

text: In DBSCAN, what are core points, border points, and noise points?
options: Noise points are the ones that form the densest most cohesive clusters
Core points have enough neighbors; border points are near core points; noise points are isolated
Core points are the extreme outliers that are furthest from all clusters
All three point types are treated exactly the same by the clustering algorithm
explanation: Core points have at least MinPts neighbors within epsilon; border points are within epsilon of a core point but have fewer neighbors; noise points are neither.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft clustering?
options: Hard assigns each point to one cluster; soft assigns probabilities of belonging to each cluster
Soft clustering does not exist as a valid machine learning technique
Hard clustering is always the strictly better approach for all problems
They are completely identical approaches with no meaningful differences
explanation: Hard clustering (e.g., K-Means) assigns each point to exactly one cluster, while soft clustering (e.g., GMM) assigns probabilities of belonging to each cluster.

text: What is the Information Criterion (BIC/AIC) used for in clustering?
options: Performing feature selection for supervised model training
Measuring the internal purity of each individual data cluster
Calculating the pairwise distances between all data points
Selecting the optimal number of clusters by balancing fit and complexity
explanation: BIC and AIC balance model fit against complexity to select the optimal number of clusters, penalizing models with more parameters.

text: What is Self-Organizing Map (SOM)?
options: An unsupervised neural network that produces a low-dimensional representation of data
A supervised classification model that predicts categorical labels
A gradient boosting algorithm that builds sequential tree models
A linear regression model that predicts continuous target values
explanation: SOM is an unsupervised neural network that maps high-dimensional data onto a low-dimensional grid while preserving topological properties.

text: How does the OPTICS algorithm improve upon DBSCAN?
options: It requires explicitly specifying the number K of clusters
It only works with fully labeled and supervised training data
It is always computationally faster than every other algorithm
It handles clusters of varying density without requiring a fixed epsilon
explanation: OPTICS (Ordering Points To Identify Clustering Structure) creates a reachability plot that allows identifying clusters at different density levels, unlike DBSCAN's fixed epsilon.

text: What is the manifold hypothesis in unsupervised learning?
options: All real-world data is always uniformly distributed in space
Data always naturally forms spherical well-separated clusters
All feature dimensions are always statistically independent
High-dimensional data often lies on a lower-dimensional manifold
explanation: The manifold hypothesis states that real-world high-dimensional data tends to concentrate near a lower-dimensional manifold, justifying dimensionality reduction techniques.

text: What is contrastive learning in the context of unsupervised representation learning?
options: A linear regression method for predicting numerical outputs
A purely supervised classification technique using labels
A density-based spatial clustering approach for grouping data
Learning representations by contrasting similar and dissimilar pairs
explanation: Contrastive learning learns representations by pulling similar (positive) pairs together and pushing dissimilar (negative) pairs apart in the embedding space.

text: Accuracy is defined as:
options: Number of correct predictions divided by total predictions
Only the count of true negative classification results
The total number of features used in the model
Only the count of true positive classification results
explanation: Accuracy = (correct predictions) / (total predictions), measuring the overall proportion of correct classifications.

text: What is a confusion matrix?
options: A Python data visualization chart library
A type of multi-layer deep neural network
A preprocessing data cleaning utility tool
A table showing predicted vs actual classifications
explanation: A confusion matrix is a table that summarizes the performance of a classification model by showing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.

text: What is precision in classification?
options: The recall evaluation metric
Overall model accuracy metric
True Positives divided by the total count
True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives)
explanation: Precision is the proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct: TP / (TP + FP).

text: What is recall (sensitivity)?
options: True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives)
True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives)
The specificity metric
The overall accuracy metric
explanation: Recall is the proportion of actual positives correctly identified: TP / (TP + FN).

text: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is used to evaluate:
options: Regression models
Classification models only
Clustering algorithms
Association rule mining
explanation: MSE measures the average squared difference between predicted and actual values, commonly used to evaluate regression models.

text: What is a training set used for?
options: Deploying the model to production
Training the model to learn patterns
Evaluating the final performance
Tuning the model hyperparameters
explanation: The training set is used to train the model by allowing it to learn patterns and relationships in the data.

text: What is a test set used for?
options: Training the model on labeled training samples
Tuning the model's hyperparameter settings
Cleaning and preprocessing the raw data
Evaluating the model's performance on unseen data
explanation: The test set evaluates how well the trained model generalizes to new, unseen data.

text: What is a false positive?
options: Making a correct and accurate model prediction
Predicting positive when the actual value is negative
Encountering a missing value in the raw data
Predicting negative when the actual value is positive
explanation: A false positive (Type I error) occurs when the model incorrectly predicts the positive class.

text: R-squared measures:
options: The proportion of variance in the target explained by the model
The total number of data samples in the dataset
The total number of input features in the training dataset
The total elapsed time required for model training
explanation: R-squared (coefficient of determination) measures how much of the variance in the dependent variable is explained by the model, ranging from 0 to 1.

text: What is a validation set?
options: The same set as the independent held-out test set
The entire full dataset including all partitions
A preprocessing data cleaning utility pipeline
A subset used to tune hyperparameters during training
explanation: A validation set is separate from training and test sets, used to tune hyperparameters and make model selection decisions during development.

text: What is the F1-score?
options: The arithmetic mean of precision and recall
The product of precision and recall values
The harmonic mean of precision and recall
The square of the model accuracy
explanation: F1-score = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall), providing a balanced measure when precision and recall are both important.

text: What does the ROC curve plot?
options: Training error values plotted against validation error values
True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at various thresholds
Precision values plotted against corresponding recall values
Training accuracy values plotted against the loss values
explanation: The ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots TPR against FPR at various classification thresholds.

text: What does AUC-ROC measure?
options: Only the precision metric of the trained classification model
Only the accuracy metric of the trained classification model
Only the recall metric of the trained classification model
The overall ability of the model to discriminate between classes
explanation: AUC-ROC (Area Under the ROC Curve) measures the model's ability to distinguish between classes across all thresholds. A value of 1.0 is perfect.

text: What is K-fold cross-validation?
options: Dividing data into K folds and using each fold as validation once
Training the model completely without any validation step
Using all available data solely for the training process
Using only two simple train-test splits for evaluation
explanation: K-fold CV splits data into K equal folds, trains K times using K-1 folds for training and the remaining fold for validation, averaging the results.

text: When is recall more important than precision?
options: When simple accuracy alone is a sufficient metric
When the dataset classes are perfectly balanced overall
When false positives are very costly to the outcome
When missing positive cases is costly, like disease detection
explanation: Recall is prioritized when false negatives are costly, such as disease screening where missing a positive case could be life-threatening.

text: What is stratified cross-validation?
options: Performing completely random splitting of the data
Ignoring overall class balance during data splitting
Using only the majority class for model training purposes
K-fold CV that preserves the class distribution in each fold
explanation: Stratified CV ensures each fold has approximately the same proportion of each class as the original dataset, important for imbalanced data.

text: What is the log loss (cross-entropy loss)?
options: A feature selection method based on mutual information scores
The exact same metric as mean squared error for regression
A metric specifically designed for evaluating cluster quality
A loss function that penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily
explanation: Log loss measures the performance of a classification model by penalizing predictions based on how far they are from the actual labels, with confident wrong predictions penalized heavily.

text: What is the purpose of a learning curve?
options: To select the most important features from the training data
To diagnose overfitting or underfitting by plotting performance vs training size
To clean and preprocess the raw input data before training
To learn about new machine learning algorithms from documentation
explanation: A learning curve plots training and validation performance as a function of training set size, helping diagnose whether the model suffers from high bias or high variance.

text: What is the difference between micro and macro averaging?
options: Micro averaging is always a better choice than macro averaging
Micro aggregates all instances globally; macro averages per-class metrics equally
Micro and macro averaging are completely identical approaches
Macro averaging ignores the overall class distribution entirely
explanation: Micro-averaging aggregates all instances globally, while macro-averaging computes metrics per class and averages them, giving equal weight to each class.

text: What is Mean Absolute Error (MAE)?
options: The single maximum prediction error value
The median of all prediction errors
The average of absolute differences between predicted and actual values
The average of all squared differences between predictions
explanation: MAE = mean(|predicted - actual|). It measures the average magnitude of errors without considering direction, less sensitive to outliers than MSE.

text: What is the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)?
options: The exact same metric as standard classification accuracy for balanced data
A metric specifically designed for evaluating unsupervised clustering
A metric that is only applicable to multi-class classification problems
A balanced metric using all four confusion matrix values, ranging from -1 to 1
explanation: MCC considers TP, TN, FP, and FN, producing a balanced measure even on imbalanced datasets. +1 is perfect, 0 is random, -1 is complete disagreement.

text: What is the difference between Type I and Type II errors?
options: The two types are completely identical errors
Type I is a false positive; Type II is a false negative
Type I is a false negative; Type II is a false positive
Both are actually true positive outcomes
explanation: Type I error (false positive) incorrectly rejects a true null hypothesis. Type II error (false negative) fails to reject a false null hypothesis.

text: What is nested cross-validation?
options: An outer CV loop for evaluation with an inner CV loop for hyperparameter tuning
A single random train-test split without any cross-validation folds
Cross-validation performed entirely without any held-out validation data
The same standard approach as regular single-loop K-fold cross-validation
explanation: Nested CV uses an outer loop for unbiased performance estimation and an inner loop for hyperparameter tuning, preventing optimistic bias from using the same data for both.

text: What is the Cohen's Kappa statistic?
options: A metric that accounts for agreement occurring by chance between predicted and actual
The exact same calculation as standard overall classification accuracy
A regression-only metric for evaluating continuous predictions
A feature importance measure for ranking input variables
explanation: Cohen's Kappa measures inter-rater agreement adjusted for chance, ranging from -1 to 1, more informative than accuracy for imbalanced datasets.

text: What is calibration in the context of model evaluation?
options: Whether predicted probabilities reflect true likelihoods of outcomes
The overall classification accuracy of the trained model
A data preprocessing step for cleaning raw data
The process of selecting the most relevant features
explanation: Calibration measures whether a model's predicted probabilities match actual frequencies. A well-calibrated model predicting 70% probability should be correct about 70% of the time.

text: What is the Brier score?
options: A feature importance measure for trees
The mean squared error of probabilistic predictions
A ranking metric for ordering search results
A clustering score for partition quality
explanation: The Brier score measures the accuracy of probabilistic predictions by computing the mean squared difference between predicted probabilities and actual outcomes (0 or 1).

text: When should you use the PR-AUC instead of ROC-AUC?
options: For evaluating unsupervised clustering partition quality
When dealing with highly imbalanced datasets where positive class is rare
When the class distribution is perfectly balanced and equal
For evaluating regression models with continuous outputs
explanation: PR-AUC is preferred for highly imbalanced datasets because ROC-AUC can be misleadingly high when the negative class dominates, while PR-AUC focuses on the minority positive class.

text: What is the purpose of bootstrapping in model evaluation?
options: Performing automated feature selection and dimensionality reduction
Estimating confidence intervals for metrics by resampling with replacement
Running hyperparameter tuning using grid or random search
Increasing the total amount of available training data samples
explanation: Bootstrapping creates multiple resampled datasets to estimate the distribution of a statistic, providing confidence intervals for model performance metrics.

text: What is the difference between leave-one-out CV and K-fold CV?
options: They are exactly identical approaches with no meaningful differences whatsoever
LOOCV is always significantly faster computationally than K-fold approaches
K-fold always produces strictly more accurate results than LOOCV methods
LOOCV uses N-1 samples for training and 1 for testing each time; K-fold uses larger folds
explanation: LOOCV is a special case where K=N (number of samples), using each sample once as validation. It has low bias but high variance and is computationally expensive.

text: What is the expected calibration error (ECE)?
options: A regression metric measuring the average absolute difference in predictions
The exact same calculation as the standard log loss or cross-entropy loss metric
The weighted average of difference between predicted confidence and actual accuracy across bins
A clustering metric measuring the silhouette score across all partitions
explanation: ECE divides predictions into bins by confidence, computes the gap between average confidence and accuracy in each bin, and takes the weighted average, measuring calibration quality.

text: What is feature engineering?
options: Building custom computer hardware for faster processing
Creating new features from existing data to improve model performance
Deleting all features from the dataset before model training
Only using the original raw data without any transformations
explanation: Feature engineering is the process of creating, transforming, or selecting features from raw data to improve ML model performance.

text: What is a feature?
options: A specific type of learning algorithm
A particular model training method
An input variable used by the model for prediction
The output produced by a trained model
explanation: A feature is an individual measurable property or attribute of the data used as input for a machine learning model.

text: What is feature selection?
options: Training the model on the full dataset
Choosing the most relevant features for the model
Deleting the entire dataset from storage
Creating new derived features from raw data
explanation: Feature selection identifies and keeps only the most relevant features, removing irrelevant or redundant ones to improve model performance.

text: What is a binary feature?
options: A continuous numeric feature
A missing feature value
A feature with many distinct categories
A feature with only two possible values (0 or 1)
explanation: A binary feature takes only two values, typically 0 and 1, representing the presence or absence of a characteristic.

text: What is a numerical feature?
options: A feature with categorical text categories
A feature containing image data
A feature containing audio data
A feature with continuous or discrete numeric values
explanation: Numerical features represent quantifiable measurements with continuous (e.g., height) or discrete (e.g., count) numeric values.

text: Feature extraction involves:
options: Deleting features from a dataset
Deriving new features from raw data
Renaming features in a table
Copying features between datasets
explanation: Feature extraction creates new features from raw data, such as extracting day of week from a date or word counts from text.

text: What does dropping a feature mean?
options: Removing a feature from the dataset
Transforming a feature with a log
Adding a brand new feature column
Scaling a feature to unit range
explanation: Dropping a feature means removing it from the dataset, typically because it is irrelevant, redundant, or harmful to model performance.

text: Why might you create interaction features?
options: To remove extreme outlier values from data
To normalize data to a standard range
To capture relationships between two or more features
To reduce the overall size of the dataset
explanation: Interaction features capture the combined effect of two or more features that may not be apparent when considering them individually.

text: What is binning?
options: Removing selected features from the dataset
Adding new synthetic features to the data
Converting continuous features into discrete intervals
Sorting data records by a column value
explanation: Binning (discretization) groups continuous values into discrete intervals or bins, like converting age into age groups.

text: What is a datetime feature?
options: A feature representing dates and times
A purely categorical feature only
A strictly binary feature only
A purely numerical feature only
explanation: Datetime features contain date/time information from which useful features like day of week, month, hour, or is_weekend can be extracted.

text: What is the purpose of polynomial features?
options: To remove unnecessary features
To normalize the data values
To handle all missing values
To capture non-linear relationships by creating powers and interactions of features
explanation: Polynomial features create new features by raising existing features to powers and computing their interactions, enabling linear models to capture non-linear patterns.

text: What is the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)?
options: An indicator for missing feature values
A measure of multicollinearity between features
A metric for overall model accuracy
A particular feature scaling method
explanation: VIF measures how much a feature's variance is inflated due to correlation with other features. High VIF (>5-10) indicates problematic multicollinearity.

text: What is target encoding?
options: Encoding categories using one-hot vector representation
Replacing categories with the mean of the target variable for that category
Removing all categorical features from dataset
Encoding using binary representation of categories
explanation: Target encoding replaces each category with the mean of the target variable for observations in that category, useful for high-cardinality features.

text: Why is feature scaling important for KNN?
options: KNN only works well with categorical type features
KNN uses distance calculations that are affected by feature magnitudes
Feature scaling actually slows down KNN
KNN actually ignores all feature scales
explanation: KNN relies on distance metrics, so features with larger scales would dominate distance calculations, making scaling essential for fair comparison.

text: What is a lag feature in time series?
options: A feature with all of its values missing
A feature created from previous time steps' values
A feature that holds only binary zero-one
A feature that is computationally slow to run
explanation: Lag features use values from previous time steps as input features, capturing temporal dependencies in time series data.

text: What is the purpose of log transformation on features?
options: To deliberately add more noise to features
To reduce skewness and handle multiplicative relationships
To intentionally increase the skewness of data
To remove all features entirely from the dataset
explanation: Log transformation reduces right skewness, compresses large values, and can linearize multiplicative relationships between features and the target.

text: What is feature hashing?
options: Encrypting feature values for secure storage and privacy compliance
Mapping high-dimensional features to a fixed-size vector using a hash function
Sorting features in the dataset by their statistical importance
Removing duplicate feature entries from the data table columns
explanation: Feature hashing maps a large number of features to a fixed-size vector using hash functions, useful for high-dimensional sparse data like text.

text: What are rolling window features?
options: Features that are created without any time component at all
Simple binary indicator features only
Static features with no temporal aspect
Features computed from a sliding window over time series data like rolling mean
explanation: Rolling window features compute statistics (mean, std, etc.) over a sliding window of past observations, capturing recent trends in time series data.

text: What is the purpose of the chi-squared test in feature selection?
options: To test the independence between categorical features and the target
To normalize all features to a common range
To impute missing values in the features
To create entirely new features from scratch
explanation: The chi-squared test measures the independence between categorical features and the target variable; features with high chi-squared values are more informative.

text: What is domain knowledge in feature engineering?
options: Using only fully automated feature methods
Deliberately ignoring the problem context entirely
Creating features completely at random each time
Using expertise about the problem area to create meaningful features
explanation: Domain knowledge leverages understanding of the specific problem area to engineer features that capture meaningful patterns, often outperforming automated approaches.

text: What is the Boruta algorithm for feature selection?
options: A multi-layer feedforward deep neural network architecture for prediction
A gradient-based linear regression method for continuous value estimation
A wrapper method using shadow features and Random Forest to find relevant features
A density-based unsupervised clustering algorithm for grouping similar data
explanation: Boruta creates shadow (randomized) copies of all features, trains a Random Forest, and compares each feature's importance to the best shadow feature to determine relevance.

text: What is the difference between wrapper, filter, and embedded feature selection?
options: Wrappers evaluate subsets with a model; filters use stats; embedded methods learn during training
Filters use trained models to evaluate each candidate feature subset iteratively
Wrappers are always strictly the best approach for every feature selection problem
They are all identical approaches that produce the exact same feature subset selections
explanation: Filter methods use statistical measures independently of the model; wrapper methods evaluate feature subsets using a model; embedded methods perform selection during model training (e.g., Lasso).

text: What is the curse of dimensionality's impact on feature engineering?
options: More features require exponentially more data to avoid sparsity
The number of dimensions has no effect on model training
Fewer features always cause the model to severely underfit
More features always improve the model performance metrics
explanation: As dimensions increase, data becomes sparse and distance metrics become less meaningful, requiring exponentially more samples to maintain statistical significance.

text: What is mutual information in feature selection?
options: A measure of the dependency between two variables based on information theory
The linear correlation coefficient between two variables
The overall variance of a single variable in a dataset
The arithmetic mean of a single variable in a dataset
explanation: Mutual information measures how much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty about another, capturing both linear and non-linear dependencies.

text: What is feature crossing in deep learning?
options: Combining two or more features to create a new feature capturing their interaction
Normalizing features to have zero mean and unit variance
Removing individual features from the dataset one at a time
Visualizing features using scatter plots and histograms
explanation: Feature crossing creates new features by combining existing ones (e.g., latitude x longitude), helping models learn interactions that may not be captured individually.

text: How does LASSO perform feature selection?
options: By using only simple correlation analysis
By removing features entirely at random
By ranking all features only by variance
By driving some feature coefficients exactly to zero through L1 regularization
explanation: LASSO's L1 penalty can shrink coefficients exactly to zero, effectively performing automatic feature selection by eliminating irrelevant features.

text: What is the difference between forward and backward feature selection?
options: Forward starts empty and adds features; backward starts with all and removes features
Forward selection actually removes features one at a time from model
Backward selection actually adds features one at a time to the model
Forward and backward selection are completely identical methods
explanation: Forward selection starts with no features and iteratively adds the most useful one. Backward elimination starts with all features and iteratively removes the least useful one.

text: What is permutation importance?
options: Simply ranking features by their alphabetical column order
A method for sorting features by their variance values
A data augmentation technique for expanding training data
Measuring importance by shuffling each feature and observing the drop in performance
explanation: Permutation importance measures how much model performance decreases when a feature's values are randomly shuffled, indicating that feature's contribution to predictions.

text: What is the concept of information leakage in feature engineering?
options: Features that are redundant copies of other existing features
Features that have missing values in some of their data records
Features containing target info that would not be available at prediction time
Features that contain a large amount of measurement noise
explanation: Information leakage occurs when features inadvertently contain information about the target that wouldn't exist in real-world prediction scenarios, leading to over-optimistic performance.

text: What is automated feature engineering (e.g., Featuretools)?
options: Generating features completely at random without any data context
Deleting features from the dataset to reduce dimensionality
Relying exclusively on manual hand-crafted feature creation processes
Using algorithms to auto-generate features from relational data via deep feature synthesis
explanation: Automated feature engineering tools like Featuretools use deep feature synthesis to automatically create features by applying transformation and aggregation primitives across related tables.

text: What is a neural network?
options: A network routing protocol for data packets
A computing system inspired by biological neural networks
A comparison-based sorting algorithm for arrays
A relational database for storing structured records
explanation: A neural network is a computational model inspired by the structure of biological neural networks, consisting of interconnected nodes (neurons).

text: What is a neuron (node) in a neural network?
options: A basic computational unit that receives inputs and produces an output
A physical data storage unit for persisting files on disk
A specific type of input feature used in the dataset
A gradient-based training algorithm for optimization
explanation: A neuron receives weighted inputs, applies an activation function, and produces an output that can be passed to other neurons.

text: What is an activation function?
options: A technique for initializing random weight parameters
A function that introduces non-linearity into the network
A function for loading data from external file sources
A method for calculating the total loss of the model
explanation: Activation functions introduce non-linearity, enabling neural networks to learn complex patterns. Examples include ReLU, sigmoid, and tanh.

text: What is a hidden layer?
options: A data preprocessing transformation layer
The very last output layer of the network
The very first input layer of the network
A layer between the input and output layers
explanation: Hidden layers are intermediate layers between input and output that perform computations and learn feature representations.

text: What is the ReLU activation function?
options: f(x) = max(0, x)
f(x) = tanh(x)
explanation: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) outputs the input if positive, otherwise zero: f(x) = max(0, x). It is the most commonly used activation function.

text: What is backpropagation?
options: A type of non-linear activation function for neurons
A specific deep neural network architecture design
A data preprocessing technique for cleaning raw inputs
An algorithm for computing gradients to update network weights
explanation: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight by propagating errors backward through the network.

text: Which framework is commonly used for deep learning?
options: NumPy only
Flask
TensorFlow
Django
explanation: TensorFlow is one of the most popular deep learning frameworks, providing tools for building and training neural networks.

text: What is an epoch in training?
options: A type of non-linear activation function node
A single individual data point in the training set
A single hidden layer within the neural network
One complete pass through the entire training dataset
explanation: An epoch is one complete iteration through the entire training dataset during the training process.

text: What is a loss function?
options: An individual input feature column used for training
A non-linear activation function applied at hidden layers
A structured collection of labeled data for evaluation
A function measuring how well predictions match actual values
explanation: A loss function quantifies the difference between predicted and actual values, guiding the optimization of model weights.

text: What is gradient descent?
options: A type of multi-layer feedforward neural network architecture with connections
An optimization algorithm minimizing loss by updating weights via steepest descent
A tree-based hierarchical data structure for nearest-neighbor lookups
A non-linear activation function that maps inputs to the range zero to one
explanation: Gradient descent iteratively adjusts model parameters in the direction that reduces the loss function most rapidly.

text: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: Gradients become very small in early layers, making training slow
Gradients become extremely large causing numerical overflow
The model has far too many hidden layers to train
The training process converges far too quickly to minimum
explanation: The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients diminish exponentially as they propagate back through many layers, hindering learning in early layers.

text: What is dropout?
options: Deleting training data samples to reduce dataset size
Randomly deactivating neurons during training to prevent overfitting
Reducing the learning rate schedule over the epochs
Removing network layers permanently from the architecture
explanation: Dropout randomly sets a fraction of neurons to zero during each training step, preventing co-adaptation and acting as regularization.

text: What is batch normalization?
options: A loss function for measuring classification prediction error
A preprocessing step applied only to the raw input data
Normalizing the inputs of each layer to stabilize and accelerate training
A type of non-linear activation function for hidden layers
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes layer inputs across a mini-batch, reducing internal covariate shift and allowing higher learning rates.

text: What is the difference between a batch and a mini-batch?
options: Batch and mini-batch gradient descent are completely identical
A batch is the full dataset; a mini-batch is a subset used per iteration
A mini-batch is always larger than a full batch of data
A batch always contains exactly one single data sample only
explanation: A batch uses the entire dataset for one update. A mini-batch uses a smaller subset, balancing computation speed and gradient accuracy.

text: What is transfer learning?
options: Moving files between different computer file systems
Transferring data records between separate databases
Using a pre-trained model on a new but related task
Training every model from scratch on random weights
explanation: Transfer learning leverages knowledge from a model pre-trained on a large dataset, fine-tuning it for a new related task with less data.

text: What is the purpose of the learning rate?
options: It determines the total number of hidden layers in the model
It explicitly sets the mini-batch size during each epoch
It defines the specific neural network architecture layout
It controls the step size of weight updates during optimization
explanation: The learning rate determines how much weights are adjusted with each update. Too high causes instability; too low causes slow convergence.

text: What is the exploding gradient problem?
options: The overall training process runs far too slowly
Gradients become extremely small, causing no weight changes
The model severely underfits the training data distribution
Gradients become extremely large, causing unstable weight updates
explanation: Exploding gradients occur when gradient values grow exponentially during backpropagation, causing numerical overflow and unstable training.

text: What is weight initialization and why is it important?
options: Setting initial weights to enable effective training and avoid gradient problems
Weights are assigned their final values only after training
Weights are always initialized to exactly zero for every neuron
The initialization strategy has no effect on the training process
explanation: Proper weight initialization (e.g., Xavier, He) ensures gradients flow well during training, preventing vanishing or exploding gradients from the start.

text: What is the Adam optimizer?
options: A non-linear activation function like sigmoid or ReLU
An adaptive learning rate optimizer combining momentum and RMSProp
A cross-entropy loss function for classification training
A type of multi-layer feedforward neural network architecture
explanation: Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) maintains per-parameter learning rates using first and second moment estimates, combining benefits of momentum and RMSProp.

text: What is a softmax output layer used for?
options: Feature extraction from the input data
Binary classification with only two output classes
Regression for continuous value prediction
Multi-class classification producing a probability distribution
explanation: A softmax output layer converts raw scores into probabilities for multi-class classification, where all class probabilities sum to 1.

text: What is the dying ReLU problem?
options: ReLU causes gradients to explode during backpropagation
Neurons permanently output zero because they get stuck in the negative region
ReLU increases peak memory usage beyond available capacity
ReLU activation is computationally too slow for large networks
explanation: Dying ReLU occurs when neurons consistently receive negative inputs, causing them to always output zero with zero gradients, effectively becoming permanently inactive.

text: What is the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis?
options: All neural network architectures are equally effective regardless of size
Sparse network architectures never work well for any real task
Dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that can match full network performance
Larger networks are always strictly better than smaller alternatives
explanation: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis posits that randomly-initialized dense networks contain sparse subnetworks ('winning tickets') that can match the full network's performance.

text: What is knowledge distillation?
options: Removing irrelevant features from the input feature space
Training a smaller student model to mimic a larger teacher model
Transferring database records between different storage systems
Augmenting the training data with synthetic generated samples
explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a compact student model to reproduce the behavior of a larger teacher model, compressing knowledge into a smaller, deployable model.

text: What is the purpose of skip connections (residual connections)?
options: Removing entire layers from the network to reduce complexity
Skipping the data preprocessing step to speed up training
Reducing the size of the training dataset to save storage
Allowing gradients to flow directly by adding input to output of a block
explanation: Skip connections add the input of a block to its output, enabling gradient flow across many layers and allowing networks to learn residual functions, as in ResNets.

text: What is gradient clipping?
options: Removing all gradients entirely from the backpropagation computation
Setting all gradient values to exactly zero after each update step
Increasing the learning rate to speed up convergence time
Limiting gradient values to a maximum threshold to prevent exploding gradients
explanation: Gradient clipping caps gradient values when they exceed a threshold, preventing exploding gradients while maintaining training stability.

text: What is the difference between SGD with momentum and Adam?
options: SGD momentum uses a fixed learning rate with velocity; Adam adapts per parameter
Adam optimizer never converges to a good solution on real data
SGD with momentum is always faster than Adam for every problem
They are completely identical optimization algorithms with no differences
explanation: SGD with momentum accumulates velocity but uses a global learning rate. Adam adapts per-parameter learning rates using running averages of gradients and squared gradients.

text: What is neural architecture search (NAS)?
options: Automated methods for finding optimal neural network architectures
Feature selection and dimensionality reduction algorithms
Manual hand-designed network architecture engineering process
Data preprocessing and cleaning pipeline automation tools
explanation: NAS uses optimization techniques to automatically discover optimal network architectures, reducing the need for manual architecture engineering.

text: What is the difference between pre-training and fine-tuning?
options: They are completely identical stages in the same training process
Fine-tuning always happens before the pre-training phase
Pre-training is designed specifically for very small datasets
Pre-training learns general representations; fine-tuning adapts to a specific task
explanation: Pre-training learns general features from large datasets. Fine-tuning adapts those learned representations to a specific downstream task with task-specific data.

text: What is the effect of batch size on training?
options: Smaller batch sizes are always strictly worse for model generalization
Larger batch sizes are always strictly better for all training scenarios
Larger batches give smoother gradients but may generalize worse; smaller batches add helpful noise
Batch size has absolutely no measurable effect on training or generalization
explanation: Larger batch sizes provide smoother gradient estimates but may converge to sharp minima that generalize poorly. Smaller batches introduce noise that can help escape sharp minima.

text: What is mixed-precision training?
options: Using both 16-bit and 32-bit floating point to speed up training while maintaining accuracy
Using only 8-bit integer arithmetic for all network computation steps
Using only 64-bit double-precision floats for maximum numerical precision
Training neural network models entirely without any GPU acceleration
explanation: Mixed-precision training uses FP16 for most computations for speed and FP32 for critical accumulations for accuracy, reducing memory usage and increasing throughput.

text: What is a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)?
options: A neural network designed for processing grid-like data such as images
A relational database system for storing structured records
A recurrent network designed exclusively for text sequence data
A comparison-based sorting algorithm for numerical arrays
explanation: CNNs use convolutional layers to automatically learn spatial hierarchies of features, making them ideal for image processing.

text: What is a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)?
options: A neural network for sequential data with memory of previous inputs
A convolutional network designed exclusively for image pixel data
An unsupervised density-based clustering algorithm for grouping
A static feedforward network with no temporal connections
explanation: RNNs process sequential data by maintaining hidden states that capture information from previous time steps.

text: What is a GAN?
options: An unsupervised clustering method based on density estimation
A specialized variant of convolutional neural networks for images
Generative Adversarial Network - two networks competing to generate realistic data
A supervised regression algorithm for predicting numeric values
explanation: A GAN consists of a generator that creates fake data and a discriminator that tries to distinguish real from fake, training adversarially.

text: What is an autoencoder?
options: A neural network that learns to compress and reconstruct its input
A comparison-based sorting algorithm for numerical arrays
A data loading utility for reading files into memory
A supervised classifier that predicts categorical labels from data
explanation: An autoencoder learns to encode input into a lower-dimensional representation and then decode it back, useful for dimensionality reduction and denoising.

text: What is LSTM?
options: Long Short-Term Memory - an RNN variant handling long-term dependencies
A cross-entropy loss function for multi-class classification
A specialized type of convolutional neural network for images
A structured data format for storing tabular information
explanation: LSTM is a special RNN architecture with gates (forget, input, output) that can learn long-term dependencies, solving the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs.

text: What is a pooling layer in a CNN?
options: A data loading layer for reading external files
The final output layer for class predictions
A layer that increases the total number of neurons
A layer that reduces spatial dimensions by down-sampling
explanation: Pooling layers reduce the spatial dimensions of feature maps (e.g., max pooling, average pooling), decreasing computation and providing translation invariance.

text: What is the purpose of a convolutional filter (kernel)?
options: To add random noise for data augmentation purposes
To store training data on disk for later retrieval access
To increase the spatial resolution of the input image
To detect specific features like edges or textures in input data
explanation: Convolutional filters slide over input data to detect local patterns like edges, textures, and shapes through learned weights.

text: What is a pre-trained model?
options: A randomly initialized model with no learned representations
A model with absolutely no prior training on any data
A model that has been permanently deleted from storage
A model already trained on a large dataset for use on new tasks
explanation: Pre-trained models have been trained on large datasets (e.g., ImageNet) and can be fine-tuned for specific tasks, saving time and data.

text: What is a generative model?
options: A model that only performs supervised categorical classification
A model that only performs numerical regression prediction
A model that learns to generate new data similar to training data
A model that only performs unsupervised data point clustering
explanation: Generative models learn the underlying distribution of training data and can generate new, similar data samples.

text: What framework was developed by Facebook (Meta) for deep learning?
options: Caffe
Keras
PyTorch
TensorFlow
explanation: PyTorch was developed by Meta (Facebook) and is widely used for deep learning research and production.

text: What is the Transformer architecture?
options: A model based on self-attention mechanisms without recurrence
A generative adversarial network for data synthesis
A density-based clustering algorithm for grouping
A standard convolutional neural network for image tasks
explanation: The Transformer uses self-attention to process all positions simultaneously, enabling parallelization and capturing long-range dependencies more effectively than RNNs.

text: What is self-attention?
options: A cross-entropy loss function that measures classification prediction error
A type of spatial pooling operation that reduces feature map dimensions
A mechanism relating different positions within a sequence to compute representations
A data augmentation method that generates synthetic training examples
explanation: Self-attention computes attention weights between all pairs of positions in a sequence, allowing each position to attend to all others for contextual understanding.

text: What is a Variational Autoencoder (VAE)?
options: A linear regression model for numeric outputs
A generative model that learns a probabilistic latent space
A standard deterministic autoencoder without sampling
A supervised classifier for categorical prediction
explanation: A VAE learns a probability distribution in the latent space, enabling generation of new data by sampling from this distribution, unlike standard autoencoders.

text: What is the difference between GRU and LSTM?
options: GRU is always more accurate than LSTM on all tasks
GRU and LSTM are completely identical architectures
GRU has fewer gates (2 vs 3) and is computationally simpler
LSTM has fewer learnable parameters than the GRU
explanation: GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) uses 2 gates (reset and update) compared to LSTM's 3 gates, making it computationally simpler while achieving comparable performance.

text: What is 1x1 convolution used for?
options: Increasing the spatial dimensions of the feature maps in the network
Changing the number of channels and adding non-linearity without changing spatial dimensions
Performing spatial pooling to reduce the feature map dimensions
Applying batch normalization across all the feature map channels
explanation: A 1x1 convolution changes the depth (number of channels) of feature maps, adds non-linearity, and reduces computation, as used in Inception networks.

text: What is depthwise separable convolution?
options: Performing a pooling operation to reduce the spatial feature dimensions
Splitting convolution into depthwise and pointwise operations to reduce computation
Applying a normalization technique to stabilize the training process
Performing a standard full convolution operation on the input feature maps
explanation: Depthwise separable convolution performs convolution on each channel independently, then combines with 1x1 convolutions, drastically reducing parameters and computation.

text: What is the encoder-decoder architecture?
options: A cross-entropy loss function for measuring prediction quality
A type of generative adversarial network with two competing agents
A structured data format for storing model configuration files
A structure where an encoder compresses input and a decoder generates output
explanation: Encoder-decoder architectures compress input into a representation (encoding) and then generate the desired output (decoding), used in translation, segmentation, and more.

text: What is data parallelism in deep learning?
options: A type of batch normalization applied across distributed nodes
Distributing training data across multiple GPUs, each running a model copy
Reducing the overall size of the training data by sampling
Using a single GPU for all training and inference computation
explanation: Data parallelism replicates the model across multiple GPUs, each processing different data batches, and synchronizes gradients for faster training.

text: What is a residual network (ResNet)?
options: A very shallow network with only a single hidden layer
A standard recurrent neural network for sequences
A deep network using skip connections that add input to output of layers
A network architecture with no inter-layer connections
explanation: ResNet introduces skip connections that bypass layers, enabling training of very deep networks (100+ layers) by mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

text: What is the purpose of attention mechanisms?
options: To allow models to focus on relevant parts of the input
To increase the mini-batch size during training
To initialize the network weights before training
To reduce the learning rate over training epochs
explanation: Attention mechanisms enable models to selectively focus on the most relevant parts of the input when producing each part of the output.

text: What is the difference between causal and bidirectional self-attention?
options: Causal attention only looks at previous positions; bidirectional looks at all positions
Bidirectional attention only looks backward at previous tokens in sequence
Causal and bidirectional attention are completely identical mechanisms
Causal attention actually looks at all positions in the input sequence
explanation: Causal (masked) attention prevents attending to future positions, used in autoregressive models like GPT. Bidirectional attention attends to all positions, used in models like BERT.

text: What is the mode collapse problem in GANs?
options: The generator produces limited variety instead of the full data distribution
The model architecture has grown far too large for memory
The discriminator fails completely and cannot distinguish anything
The training process runs far too slowly to converge in time
explanation: Mode collapse occurs when the generator learns to produce only a few types of outputs that fool the discriminator, failing to capture the full diversity of the data distribution.

text: What is the Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) and how does it improve training?
options: It uses only convolutional layers without any dense layers
It uses the Wasserstein distance for more stable training gradients
It completely removes the discriminator from the architecture
It uses standard cross-entropy loss for the discriminator
explanation: WGAN uses the Wasserstein (Earth Mover's) distance instead of JS divergence, providing smoother gradients even when distributions don't overlap, leading to more stable training.

text: What is Neural ODE?
options: Modeling continuous-depth neural networks using ordinary differential equations
A generative adversarial network variant for image synthesis
A standard discrete-layer feedforward neural network architecture
An unsupervised density-based technique for data clustering
explanation: Neural ODEs treat hidden state dynamics as continuous-time ODEs solved with ODE solvers, enabling adaptive computation and continuous-depth networks.

text: What is the Flash Attention algorithm?
options: An IO-aware exact attention algorithm reducing memory from quadratic to linear
A data loading technique for prefetching training batches from disk
An approximate attention method that trades accuracy for modest speed
A new non-linear activation function for transformer hidden layers
explanation: Flash Attention computes exact attention by tiling and recomputation, reducing GPU memory reads/writes and achieving linear memory complexity while being faster than standard attention.

text: What is the difference between model parallelism and data parallelism?
options: Data parallelism splits the model layers across devices for training
Model parallelism splits the model across devices; data parallelism replicates model with split data
Model parallelism splits the data while keeping the full model on one device
They are completely identical distributed training strategies with no differences
explanation: Data parallelism replicates the model on each device with different data. Model parallelism distributes different parts of the model across devices, needed when the model doesn't fit on one device.

text: What is the concept of equivariance in CNNs?
options: Output is always identical regardless of any transformation of input
All layers in the network have exactly equal learned weight values
Output transforms in the same way as the input (e.g., shift in input causes shift in output)
The neural network architecture is perfectly symmetric across all layers
explanation: Equivariance means if the input is transformed (e.g., translated), the output transforms correspondingly. CNNs are translation equivariant in convolutional layers.

text: What is a diffusion model?
options: A recurrent neural network architecture for sequence modeling
An unsupervised density-based clustering algorithm for data
A generative model that learns to reverse a gradual noising process
A generative adversarial network variant using two competing agents
explanation: Diffusion models generate data by learning to reverse a gradual process of adding noise to data, achieving state-of-the-art results in image generation.

text: What is the Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture?
options: A standard model ensemble using simple majority voting
A data preprocessing method for feature normalization
A single monolithic large network without any routing
A model using a gating network to route inputs to specialized sub-networks
explanation: MoE uses a gating mechanism to dynamically route each input to a subset of specialized expert networks, enabling larger model capacity with manageable computation.

text: What is quantization in deep learning?
options: Increasing the overall model size by adding more trainable parameters
Applying data augmentation transforms to expand the training set
Reducing weight and activation precision to lower bit representations for efficiency
Adding more hidden layers to increase the depth of the network
explanation: Quantization converts model weights and activations from high-precision (FP32) to lower-precision formats (INT8, INT4), reducing model size and inference latency with minimal accuracy loss.

text: What does NLP stand for?
options: Numerical Linear Processing
Network Logic Protocol
Natural Language Processing
Neural Language Programming
explanation: NLP stands for Natural Language Processing, the field of AI dealing with interaction between computers and human language.

text: Tokenization in NLP means:
options: Encrypting text for secure data transmission
Splitting text into individual words or subwords
Deleting text from the data pipeline
Compressing text to reduce storage size
explanation: Tokenization breaks text into smaller units (tokens) such as words, subwords, or characters for processing by NLP models.

text: What is sentiment analysis?
options: Summarizing long documents into shorter text passages
Translating text from one natural language to another language
Generating entirely new text from a given prompt input
Determining the emotional tone of text (positive, negative, neutral)
explanation: Sentiment analysis identifies the emotional tone or opinion expressed in text, classifying it as positive, negative, or neutral.

text: Stop words are:
options: Important domain-specific keywords carrying high information
Common words like 'the' and 'is' often removed in processing
Rare words that appear only once in the entire corpus
Technical terms from a specialized subject vocabulary
explanation: Stop words are frequently occurring words that carry little meaning and are often removed during text preprocessing to focus on content words.

text: What is text classification?
options: Generating entirely new text from a prompt
Translating text between different languages
Editing and correcting text for grammar
Assigning predefined categories to text documents
explanation: Text classification assigns predefined labels or categories to text documents, such as spam detection or topic classification.

text: What is a corpus in NLP?
options: A large collection of text documents
A specific grammar rule definition
A single word in a sentence
A type of neural network model
explanation: A corpus is a large, structured collection of text documents used for training and evaluating NLP models.

text: What is stemming?
options: Reducing words to their root form by removing suffixes
Adding prefixes to words for morphological expansion
Counting the total frequency of words in text
Translating words between two different languages
explanation: Stemming removes suffixes to reduce words to their root form (e.g., 'running' -> 'run'), simplifying text for processing.

text: What is lemmatization?
options: Counting the total characters in a word
Removing all vowels from the input text
Adding suffixes for morphological inflection
Reducing words to their base dictionary form
explanation: Lemmatization reduces words to their base dictionary form (lemma) using vocabulary and morphological analysis (e.g., 'better' -> 'good').

text: Named Entity Recognition (NER) identifies:
options: Grammar errors found throughout the given text
Spelling mistakes identified within the text body
The overall length of each sentence in the document
Named entities like people, organizations, and locations in text
explanation: NER identifies and classifies named entities in text into predefined categories such as person, organization, location, and date.

text: What is machine translation?
options: Performing manual text translation
Generating new text from a prompt
Summarizing text into shorter form
Automatically translating text from one language to another
explanation: Machine translation uses AI to automatically translate text between languages, as in Google Translate.

text: What is TF-IDF?
options: A recurrent neural network variant designed for sequential text modeling
A structured query language for relational database data retrieval
A multi-layer deep feedforward neural network architecture for classification
A weighting scheme reflecting word importance in a document relative to a corpus
explanation: TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) weighs words by how often they appear in a document relative to how common they are across all documents.

text: What is Word2Vec?
options: A simple word frequency counter for bag-of-words features
A technique learning dense vector representations of words from context
A language dictionary for looking up word definitions
A rule-based grammar checker for text correction
explanation: Word2Vec learns dense vector representations (embeddings) of words where semantically similar words are mapped to nearby points in vector space.

text: What is the bag-of-words model?
options: Representing text with full word order preserved
A syntactic parsing technique for sentences
Representing text as the frequency of words, ignoring order
A neural language model using word embeddings
explanation: Bag-of-words represents text as a vector of word frequencies, disregarding grammar and word order while capturing word presence.

text: What is BERT?
options: A rule-based expert system for language processing
A generative adversarial network designed for text data
A pre-trained transformer for bidirectional language understanding
A type of recurrent neural network for sequential processing
explanation: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is pre-trained on masked language modeling and next sentence prediction for bidirectional context understanding.

text: What is attention in sequence-to-sequence models?
options: A data augmentation method for creating synthetic text training data
A mechanism allowing the decoder to focus on relevant parts of the input sequence
A cross-entropy loss function for measuring sequence prediction accuracy
A regularization technique for preventing overfitting in sequence models
explanation: Attention allows the decoder to attend to different parts of the input sequence when generating each output token, improving translation and generation quality.

text: What is text embedding?
options: Converting text into dense numerical vectors
Removing text from the raw dataset
Encrypting text for secure storage
Converting numbers into readable text
explanation: Text embedding converts text into dense numerical vector representations that capture semantic meaning, enabling mathematical operations on text.

text: What is part-of-speech (POS) tagging?
options: Removing individual words from the input text sequence
Translating text between two different natural languages
Assigning grammatical categories (noun, verb, etc.) to each word
Counting the number of syllables in each word
explanation: POS tagging assigns grammatical labels like noun, verb, adjective to each word in a sentence, aiding in syntactic analysis.

text: What is sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) modeling?
options: Mapping a single input word to a single corresponding output word
Sorting input sequences into a predefined correct order
Clustering multiple sequences into meaningful related groups
Mapping an input sequence to an output sequence of potentially different length
explanation: Seq2Seq models transform an input sequence into an output sequence, used in translation, summarization, and dialogue generation.

text: What is cosine similarity used for in NLP?
options: Counting the total frequency of words in text
Parsing sentences into syntax tree structures
Generating new text from a trained model
Measuring the similarity between two text vectors
explanation: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, indicating how similar two texts are regardless of their magnitude.

text: What is a language model?
options: A static vocabulary dictionary for word lookup
A rule-based grammar checker for syntax only
A database of aligned translation memory pairs
A model that predicts the probability of a sequence of words
explanation: A language model estimates the probability distribution over sequences of words, used for text generation, completion, and evaluation of sentence fluency.

text: What is the difference between GPT and BERT architectures?
options: Both use exactly the same attention mechanism and training objective
GPT uses bidirectional attention; BERT uses causal left-to-right attention
They are completely identical transformer architectures with no differences
GPT uses causal attention for generation; BERT uses bidirectional attention for understanding
explanation: GPT uses masked (causal) self-attention for autoregressive text generation. BERT uses bidirectional self-attention for understanding tasks, seeing context from both directions.

text: What is byte-pair encoding (BPE) in tokenization?
options: A tokenization approach that only works at the word level
A tokenization strategy that works at sentence level
A tokenization method operating at character level only
A subword tokenization algorithm that iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs
explanation: BPE starts with characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to create subword units, balancing vocabulary size and out-of-vocabulary handling.

text: What is the perplexity metric in language models?
options: The total elapsed time required for training the model
The number of parameters or total size of the model
A measure of how well a model predicts a sample; lower is better
The total number of words in the vocabulary of the model
explanation: Perplexity measures how well a probability distribution predicts a sample. Lower perplexity indicates the model is better at predicting the text.

text: What is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)?
options: Combining retrieval with generation to ground outputs in external knowledge
An unsupervised clustering method for grouping similar documents
A purely generative approach relying entirely on internal knowledge
A purely retrieval approach returning documents without generation
explanation: RAG retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and uses them as context for the language model's generation, reducing hallucination and enabling knowledge updates.

text: What is the problem of hallucination in large language models?
options: Generating plausible-sounding but factually incorrect or fabricated information
Using too much GPU memory during the inference computation
Producing completely empty outputs with no generated tokens
Generating text output at an excessively slow processing speed
explanation: Hallucination refers to LLMs generating confident, fluent text that contains fabricated facts, incorrect details, or unsupported claims.

text: What is the concept of positional encoding in Transformers?
options: Normalizing the positional coordinates of data in feature space
Encoding the physical position of GPUs in a server rack cluster
Adding position info to embeddings since Transformers lack inherent sequence order
Sorting the input data samples by their index in the dataset
explanation: Since Transformers process all positions in parallel (no recurrence), positional encodings add sequence order information to input embeddings using sinusoidal functions or learned embeddings.

text: What is cross-lingual transfer learning?
options: Translating all text first then training on the translated version
Ignoring language differences and treating all text the same
Training on one language and applying knowledge to another language
Training separate independent models for each individual language
explanation: Cross-lingual transfer leverages knowledge learned from one language to improve performance on another, often using multilingual pre-trained models like mBERT or XLM-R.

text: What is the difference between extractive and abstractive summarization?
options: Extractive generates completely new text from the source material
Abstractive only selects existing sentences from the original text
Extractive and abstractive summarization are identical methods
Extractive selects existing sentences; abstractive generates new sentences
explanation: Extractive summarization selects key sentences from the source text. Abstractive summarization generates new sentences that capture the main ideas, potentially using words not in the original.

text: What is prompt engineering?
options: Cleaning and preprocessing the raw input training data
Modifying the internal neural network model architecture
Training the language model from scratch on new domain data
Designing effective input prompts to guide LLM behavior without fine-tuning
explanation: Prompt engineering crafts input prompts that effectively guide pre-trained language models to produce desired outputs without modifying model weights.

text: What is the RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) technique?
options: Unsupervised pre-training using masked language modeling objectives
Standard supervised fine-tuning using labeled input-output training pairs
Data augmentation using paraphrasing and back-translation methods
Fine-tuning language models using human preference judgments as reward signals
explanation: RLHF trains a reward model from human preference comparisons and uses reinforcement learning to fine-tune the language model to generate outputs aligned with human preferences.

text: Computer vision enables computers to:
options: Interpret and understand visual information from images and videos
Design and build responsive web page user interfaces
Manage and query relational database table records
Process only natural language text data and documents
explanation: Computer vision is a field of AI that enables computers to extract meaningful information from images, videos, and other visual inputs.

text: What is image classification?
options: Segmenting an image into parts
Assigning a label to an entire image
Detecting objects in a given image
Generating an image from scratch
explanation: Image classification assigns a single category label to an entire image based on its visual content.

text: What is a pixel?
options: An input feature column name
A gradient training algorithm
A type of deep neural network
The smallest unit of a digital image
explanation: A pixel (picture element) is the smallest addressable element of a digital image, with color values representing visual information.

text: What is object detection?
options: Removing detected objects from an existing image frame
Classifying the entire image into a single category label
Identifying and locating objects within an image with bounding boxes
Generating entirely new photorealistic synthetic images
explanation: Object detection identifies what objects are present in an image and where they are located, typically using bounding boxes.

text: RGB stands for:
options: Random, Generated, Binary
Recursive, Generative, Base
Real, Gradient, Bias
Red, Green, Blue
explanation: RGB (Red, Green, Blue) is the standard color model for digital images, where each pixel has three channel values.

text: What is image segmentation?
options: Resizing an image to different specified dimensions
Partitioning an image into meaningful regions at the pixel level
Rotating an image by a specified number of degrees
Cropping an image to a smaller rectangular area
explanation: Image segmentation assigns a label to every pixel in an image, dividing it into meaningful regions or objects.

text: What does a convolution operation do to an image?
options: Applies a filter to extract features like edges and textures
Increases the image resolution to a higher pixel count
Changes the file format of the image container file
Deletes the image entirely from disk storage space
explanation: Convolution slides a filter over the image, computing element-wise multiplications and sums to detect local features like edges, corners, and textures.

text: What is a grayscale image?
options: An image with only shades of gray (single channel)
A volumetric three-dimensional image stack
A binary black and white image with two values
A full-color image with three RGB channels
explanation: A grayscale image has a single channel where each pixel represents intensity from black (0) to white (255).

text: What is image resizing?
options: Rotating the image by a specified angle value
Changing the dimensions (width and height) of an image
Deleting the image from the disk file system
Changing the color space of the image channels
explanation: Image resizing changes the width and height of an image, often required to match the input dimensions expected by neural networks.

text: Face detection is an example of:
options: Reinforcement learning
Computer vision
Data analytics
Natural language processing
explanation: Face detection uses computer vision techniques to identify and locate human faces in images or video.

text: What is the difference between semantic and instance segmentation?
options: Instance segmentation completely ignores the object class labels
Semantic segmentation is always the better approach overall
Semantic labels every pixel by class; instance distinguishes individual objects of the same class
Semantic and instance segmentation are completely identical approaches
explanation: Semantic segmentation labels each pixel with a class but doesn't distinguish between instances. Instance segmentation identifies each individual object separately.

text: What is data augmentation in computer vision?
options: Deleting images from the dataset to make it smaller and more manageable
Reducing the overall image quality to speed up the training process
Applying transformations like rotation, flipping, and cropping to increase training data
Converting all images to grayscale only without any other transformation
explanation: Data augmentation applies random transformations (rotation, flipping, scaling, color jittering) to training images, increasing dataset diversity and reducing overfitting.

text: What is the YOLO algorithm?
options: A pixel-level semantic image segmentation algorithm
A supervised multi-class image classification algorithm
You Only Look Once - a real-time object detection algorithm
An unsupervised K-means image clustering algorithm
explanation: YOLO processes the entire image in a single pass through the network, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities simultaneously for real-time object detection.

text: What is image feature extraction?
options: Adding textual annotation overlays onto the image pixels
Compressing images into smaller file archive formats
Identifying and representing distinctive visual patterns in images
Deleting specific features or layers from the image data
explanation: Feature extraction identifies meaningful visual patterns (edges, textures, shapes) that can be represented as numerical features for downstream tasks.

text: What is the purpose of max pooling?
options: Adding random noise to the feature map pixel values
Changing the color representation of the image data
Reducing spatial dimensions while retaining the most prominent features
Increasing the spatial size of the feature map dimensions
explanation: Max pooling selects the maximum value in each pooling window, reducing spatial dimensions while preserving the most activated features and providing some translation invariance.

text: What is optical flow?
options: A type of spatial convolutional image filter kernel operation
A standard color model representation for digital images
The pattern of apparent motion of objects between consecutive frames
A container file format for storing compressed video data
explanation: Optical flow estimates the motion of pixels between consecutive video frames, used in video analysis, action recognition, and motion tracking.

text: What is the IoU (Intersection over Union) metric?
options: A metric measuring overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes
A metric that measures overall image quality and clarity
A metric that assesses the overall color accuracy of images
A metric for evaluating the spatial resolution of images
explanation: IoU calculates the ratio of the intersection area to the union area of predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, commonly used to evaluate object detection.

text: What are anchor boxes in object detection?
