explanation: A validation set is used to tune hyperparameters and make modeling decisions during development, separate from the final test set.

text: What does underfitting indicate about a model?
options: The model is too simple to capture patterns
The model uses too many features
The model has been trained too long
The model is too complex for the data
explanation: Underfitting indicates the model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data, resulting in poor performance on both training and test data.

text: Why is accuracy a poor metric for imbalanced classification problems?
options: Accuracy requires balanced classes and cannot be computed on imbalanced data sets
Accuracy is only defined for regression problems and not for classification tasks
A model predicting only the majority class achieves high accuracy despite being useless
Accuracy always gives a value of zero when classes are imbalanced in the dataset
explanation: On imbalanced data, a model predicting only the majority class achieves high accuracy while completely failing on the minority class.

text: What is the ROC curve and what does it visualize?
options: A plot of training loss versus validation loss over each training epoch number
A plot of precision versus recall for different classification threshold values
A plot of model complexity versus generalization error for model selection use
A plot of true positive rate versus false positive rate at different thresholds
explanation: The ROC curve plots TPR against FPR at various classification thresholds, showing the tradeoff between sensitivity and specificity.

text: How does K-fold cross-validation improve upon a simple train-test split?
options: It randomly assigns labels to the test set to simulate real-world data distribution
It always uses exactly two folds regardless of the K value specified by the user
It uses all data for both training and testing reducing variance of performance estimate
It eliminates the need for any test data by using only training data for evaluation
explanation: K-fold CV uses all data for both training and testing across K iterations, reducing the variance of performance estimates compared to a single split.

text: What is the difference between micro and macro averaging for multi-class metrics?
options: Micro averages per class then takes mean while macro aggregates all predictions globally
Micro aggregates predictions globally while macro averages per class metrics equally
Micro averaging is used for binary classification while macro is for multi-class only
Both micro and macro averaging always produce exactly identical metric values here
explanation: Micro-averaging aggregates all predictions globally (favoring majority classes), while macro-averaging computes per-class metrics and averages them equally.

text: What does the Area Under the ROC Curve represent?
options: The probability a random positive sample is ranked higher than a random negative one
The exact accuracy of the model at the optimal classification threshold selected
The maximum number of features the model can effectively use for prediction
The total training time required to achieve the best validation set performance
explanation: AUC represents the probability that the model ranks a randomly chosen positive sample higher than a randomly chosen negative one.

text: When should you use stratified K-fold cross-validation instead of standard K-fold?
options: When class distributions are imbalanced to ensure each fold represents all classes
When all features are categorical and need special handling during evaluation
When the dataset is very large and standard K-fold is computationally too expensive
When the dataset has perfectly balanced class proportions across all groups
explanation: Stratified K-fold preserves class proportions in each fold, important for imbalanced datasets where standard K-fold might miss minority classes in some folds.

text: What is the bias-variance tradeoff in model evaluation?
options: Bias and variance are independent and changing one has no effect on the other one
Higher bias always leads to higher variance and both increase simultaneously always
The tradeoff only applies to neural networks and not to any traditional ML algorithms
Simpler models have high bias low variance while complex models have low bias high variance
explanation: Simple models underfit (high bias, low variance), complex models overfit (low bias, high variance). The goal is to find the optimal complexity balancing both.

text: What is the purpose of a learning curve in model evaluation?
options: To visualize the feature importance rankings from a trained random forest model
To display the optimal hyperparameters found during grid search tuning process
To show how training and validation performance change with training set size
To plot the distribution of prediction errors across all test set observations
explanation: Learning curves plot performance versus training set size, helping diagnose whether adding more data or increasing complexity would improve results.

text: How does the log loss metric differ from accuracy for evaluation?
options: Log loss and accuracy always produce identical rankings of model performance
Log loss can only be used with regression models while accuracy is for classifiers
Log loss measures prediction speed while accuracy measures correctness of results
Log loss penalizes confident wrong predictions more heavily than accuracy does
explanation: Log loss penalizes confident incorrect predictions more heavily, providing a nuanced evaluation of probability estimates rather than just correct/incorrect counts.

text: What is the purpose of a calibration curve in model evaluation?
options: To assess whether predicted probabilities match actual observed frequencies
To determine the optimal number of features to include in the final model
To measure how fast the model makes predictions on new test data samples
To visualize the convergence of the training loss during model optimization
explanation: A calibration curve plots predicted probabilities against actual frequencies, showing whether the model's probability estimates are trustworthy.

text: In medical diagnosis, why is recall often prioritized over precision?
options: Recall is always higher than precision so it is the easier metric to optimize for
Missing a positive diagnosis is more dangerous than a false alarm in many cases
Medical models always achieve perfect precision making recall the only variable metric
Precision is only relevant for non-medical applications and cannot be used in health
explanation: In medical diagnosis, false negatives (missed diseases) can be life-threatening, making recall critical. False positives lead to additional tests, which is less harmful.

text: Why can nested cross-validation be necessary for unbiased model selection and evaluation?
options: Nested cross-validation is only needed for datasets with fewer than fifty observations
Hyperparameter tuning on the same CV folds used for evaluation causes optimistic bias
Standard cross-validation already provides completely unbiased model selection results
It is a deprecated technique that has been replaced by simple holdout validation now
explanation: Standard CV with hyperparameter tuning on the same folds leaks information, causing optimistic evaluation. Nested CV uses separate inner (tuning) and outer (evaluation) loops.

text: A model achieves 0.95 AUC but poor performance at the deployed threshold. What is the likely issue?
options: The model was trained on too much data causing it to generalize too well overall
The model is well-calibrated but the AUC metric was computed with an error here
AUC summarizes all thresholds but the operational threshold may fall in a poor region
An AUC of 0.95 guarantees excellent performance at every possible threshold value
explanation: AUC averages performance across all thresholds, but the specific operational threshold may fall in a region where the model performs poorly. Threshold-specific metrics matter.

text: When comparing models, why is statistical significance testing important beyond comparing mean metrics?
options: Statistical testing is only applicable to models trained on datasets exceeding millions
Performance differences may be due to random variation rather than true model superiority
Mean metrics always provide sufficient information to select the best model reliably
It is never needed because cross-validation already accounts for all statistical variance
explanation: Observed performance differences may be due to random data variation. Statistical tests determine whether differences are significant or likely due to chance.

text: What is the Matthews Correlation Coefficient and why is it preferred for imbalanced datasets?
options: It is identical to accuracy and provides no additional information for imbalanced data
It only considers true positives and ignores all other confusion matrix cell values
It uses all confusion matrix values providing balanced evaluation even with skewed classes
It requires balanced classes to compute and returns undefined for imbalanced datasets
explanation: MCC uses all four confusion matrix values (TP, TN, FP, FN), producing a balanced metric from -1 to +1 that is informative even with severe class imbalance.

text: How does the precision-recall curve complement the ROC curve for rare event detection?
options: Both curves always provide identical information regardless of the class balance ratio
PR curves better expose model performance on the minority class that ROC may obscure
ROC curves are always superior to PR curves in every evaluation scenario encountered
PR curves can only be computed when classes are perfectly balanced in the dataset
explanation: For rare events, ROC curves can appear optimistic because TNs dominate. PR curves focus on positive class performance, better revealing classifier behavior on minority class.

text: Why might a model perform well in cross-validation but poorly in real-world deployment?
options: Distribution shift between training data and production data degrades model performance
Cross-validation uses test data for training which inflates deployment expectations
Models that perform well in CV are guaranteed to perform well in any deployment setup
Cross-validation always provides perfectly accurate estimates of deployment performance
explanation: Real-world deployment may involve data drift, different populations, or distribution shift that cross-validation on historical data does not capture.

text: What is the purpose of the DeLong test in comparing ROC curves of two models?
options: It statistically tests whether two AUC values are significantly different from each other
It measures the calibration quality of both models probability estimates simultaneously
It determines whether one model trains faster than the other on the same dataset
It automatically selects the optimal threshold for deploying both models in production
explanation: The DeLong test provides a statistical comparison of two correlated AUC values, determining if the difference is statistically significant.

text: In time-series model evaluation, why is standard K-fold cross-validation inappropriate?
options: Standard K-fold is actually the recommended approach for all time-series evaluations
Random fold assignment violates temporal ordering and leaks future data into training
Time-series data always has too few samples for any cross-validation method to work
K-fold cross-validation can only be applied to classification not regression problems
explanation: Standard K-fold randomly mixes temporal data, allowing future information to leak into training folds. Time-series requires forward-chaining or expanding window CV.

text: How does Bayesian evaluation of classifiers improve upon frequentist hypothesis testing?
options: It provides probability distributions over performance differences enabling richer analysis
Bayesian evaluation only works with neural network classifiers and no other methods
It eliminates the need for any test data by computing performance from prior alone
Bayesian methods always agree with frequentist tests making them completely redundant
explanation: Bayesian evaluation provides full posterior distributions over performance differences, giving probabilities of practical significance rather than binary accept/reject decisions.

text: What is feature engineering in machine learning?
options: Evaluating model performance
Training a model automatically
Creating informative input variables
Deploying models to production
explanation: Feature engineering is the process of creating, transforming, and selecting input variables to improve machine learning model performance.

text: What is feature selection?
options: Adding all available features to the model without any filtering
Converting feature names from one language to another one
Generating entirely new features from random noise data
Choosing the most relevant features for model training use
explanation: Feature selection identifies and retains only the most relevant features, removing irrelevant or redundant ones to improve model performance.

text: What does one-hot encoding create from a categorical feature?
options: A single numerical column
A text description field
Multiple binary columns
A sorted ordinal value
explanation: One-hot encoding creates a binary column for each category, with 1 indicating the presence of that category and 0 otherwise.

text: What is the purpose of scaling numerical features?
options: To add missing value indicators
To bring features to a similar range
To change the data type of features
To remove features from the data
explanation: Feature scaling brings numerical features to a similar range, preventing features with larger scales from dominating the model.

text: Which technique creates new features by combining existing ones?
options: Feature deletion
Feature encryption
Feature interaction
Feature imputation
explanation: Feature interaction creates new features by combining existing ones through operations like multiplication, addition, or ratios.

text: What is binning used for in feature engineering?
options: Encrypting sensitive data for privacy
Converting categories into continuous values
Removing duplicate values from dataset
Converting continuous variables into categories
explanation: Binning converts continuous variables into discrete categories by grouping values into intervals or bins.

text: What does a polynomial feature transformation create?
options: Compressed versions of original data
Lower dimensional representations
Higher order combinations of features
Random noise features for testing
explanation: Polynomial features create higher-order combinations (squares, cubes, interactions) of existing features to capture non-linear relationships.

text: What is the difference between ordinal and nominal encoding?
options: Ordinal preserves category order while nominal treats categories as equal
Ordinal only works with numbers while nominal only works with strings
Both encodings are identical and always produce the same output values
Ordinal treats categories as equal while nominal preserves their ordering
explanation: Ordinal encoding preserves the natural order of categories (low/medium/high), while nominal encoding treats all categories as unordered.

text: What is a derived feature?
options: An original feature from raw data
A new feature created from existing ones
A feature with many missing values
A feature removed during cleaning
explanation: A derived feature is a new variable created by transforming or combining existing features, like extracting the year from a date.

text: Why is feature engineering important for model performance?
options: It always makes models train faster
It removes the requirement for training data
It eliminates the need for model tuning
Good features can significantly improve accuracy
explanation: Good feature engineering often has a larger impact on model performance than algorithm choice, providing the model with more informative inputs.

text: What is the wrapper method for feature selection?
options: Selecting features alphabetically by their column name in the original data frame
Using a model to evaluate different feature subsets and selecting the best one found
Selecting features based only on their individual correlation with the target variable
Removing all features with missing values regardless of their predictive importance
explanation: Wrapper methods use a predictive model to evaluate different feature subsets, selecting the combination that optimizes model performance.

text: How does target encoding work for high-cardinality categorical features?
options: It replaces each category with the mean of the target variable for that group
It replaces each category with a randomly assigned numerical identifier value
It creates one binary column for each unique category in the feature column
It removes all categories that appear fewer than ten times in the dataset
explanation: Target encoding replaces each category with the mean target value for that category, creating a compact numerical representation for high-cardinality features.

text: What is the purpose of creating interaction features between variables?
options: To convert all numerical features into categorical features for tree models
To reduce the total number of features in the dataset to save memory space
To capture combined effects that individual features alone cannot represent
To remove multicollinearity between features by merging them into one column
explanation: Interaction features capture combined effects (like price x quantity = revenue) that individual features alone cannot represent, often improving model performance.

text: Why is the filter method for feature selection computationally efficient?
options: It requires training the model multiple times with different feature subsets each
It evaluates features independently of any model using statistical measures only
It uses a complex model to evaluate every possible subset of feature combinations
It only works with datasets that have fewer than one hundred features total
explanation: Filter methods use statistical measures (correlation, mutual information, chi-squared) independent of any model, making them fast even for high-dimensional data.

text: What is the benefit of creating date-based features from timestamps?
options: Machine learning algorithms automatically extract date features without engineering
Date-based features always decrease model accuracy compared to raw timestamps
Extracting components like day of week and month reveals cyclical patterns in data
Timestamps are already in the optimal format for all machine learning algorithms
explanation: Extracting components like day of week, month, hour, and is_weekend from timestamps reveals cyclical and seasonal patterns that raw timestamps cannot convey.

text: How does the Variance Threshold method work for feature selection?
options: It increases the variance of low-variance features through data augmentation
It selects features with the highest variance above the target variable mean
It calculates the variance between features to detect multicollinearity issues
It removes features with variance below a specified threshold as uninformative
explanation: Variance Threshold removes features with variance below a specified cutoff, as low-variance features carry little discriminative information.

text: What is feature hashing and when is it useful?
options: It sorts features by importance and removes the least important ones from data
It encrypts feature values for security during model training in production use
It maps high-dimensional categorical features to fixed-size vectors using a hash
It creates polynomial features from the original feature set for better models
explanation: Feature hashing maps categories to a fixed-size vector using a hash function, useful for very high-cardinality features where one-hot encoding is impractical.

text: Why might you apply a log transformation to a highly skewed feature?
options: It removes all outliers from the feature by compressing them to zero values
Log transformation converts categorical features to numerical features directly
Log transformation always improves model accuracy regardless of distribution shape
It reduces skewness and helps satisfy normality assumptions of some algorithms
explanation: Log transformation reduces right skewness, bringing distributions closer to normal and helping algorithms that assume normally distributed features.

text: What is the embedded method for feature selection?
options: Selecting features by running the model on every possible subset combination
Selecting features before training any model using only statistical measures
Feature selection performed as part of the model training process itself directly
Removing all features except the one with the highest correlation to target
explanation: Embedded methods perform feature selection during model training (e.g., L1 regularization, tree-based importance), combining the efficiency of filters with model awareness.

text: How do you handle cyclical features like hour of day or day of week?
options: Treat them as regular continuous numerical features without any transformation
Simply remove cyclical features since they do not contain useful information
Encode them using sine and cosine transformations to preserve cyclic continuity
Convert them to categorical features and apply one-hot encoding to each value
explanation: Sine and cosine encoding preserves the cyclical nature (e.g., hour 23 is close to hour 0) that linear numerical encoding would lose.

text: Why can target encoding cause data leakage and how is it typically mitigated?
options: It uses target values during encoding so leave-one-out or fold-based encoding prevents leak
Target encoding never causes data leakage so no mitigation is ever required
Data leakage only occurs with one-hot encoding and never with target encoding method
Target encoding automatically prevents leakage through its internal smoothing mechanism
explanation: Target encoding uses the target variable to create features, leaking information. Leave-one-out encoding or fold-based encoding prevents the current sample's target from influencing its encoding.

text: When engineering features for a gradient boosted tree model, why is binning continuous features usually unnecessary?
options: Tree-based models already find optimal split points so binning loses useful granularity
Gradient boosted trees cannot process binned features and require raw continuous input
Binning always improves gradient boosted tree performance without any exceptions
Binning is only useful for neural networks and cannot be applied to any tree models
explanation: Gradient boosted trees naturally find optimal split points during training, so pre-binning loses granularity that the model could exploit.

text: How does autoML automate feature engineering and what are its limitations?
options: AutoML perfectly replaces all manual feature engineering without any limitations ever
AutoML only works with image data and cannot handle tabular or text feature engineering
It explores transformations automatically but may miss domain-specific meaningful features
It always produces fewer features than manual engineering making it strictly inferior
explanation: AutoML can discover transformations automatically, but domain knowledge often yields features (like specific business ratios) that automated searches cannot find.

text: Why might feature engineering differ between linear models and tree-based models?
options: Tree-based models require normalized features while linear models handle raw values
Both model types benefit from exactly identical feature engineering approaches always
Linear models need non-linear transformations while trees handle non-linearity natively
Feature engineering is only needed for tree-based models and never for linear models
explanation: Linear models need explicit non-linear transformations and interactions, while trees learn these automatically. Trees don't need scaling but may need cardinality reduction.

text: What is the risk of using highly engineered features that are specific to the training distribution?
options: Engineered features are always more robust than raw features in all scenarios tested
The risk is minimal because feature engineering always reduces overfitting in models
They may not generalize to production data if the distribution shifts over time period
Highly engineered features always generalize perfectly to any future data distribution
explanation: Features engineered for training distribution may break under distribution shift. Robust engineering considers potential changes and avoids overly data-specific transformations.

text: How does mutual information differ from Pearson correlation for feature selection?
options: Pearson captures non-linear relationships while mutual information is limited to linear
Both methods measure identical relationships and always produce the same feature ranking
Mutual information only works with binary features while Pearson works with all types
Mutual information captures any dependency while Pearson only detects linear relationships
explanation: Mutual information captures any statistical dependency (linear, non-linear, complex), while Pearson correlation only measures linear relationships between variables.

text: In NLP, why might TF-IDF features outperform raw word count features for text classification?
options: Raw word counts always outperform TF-IDF so this premise is incorrect in all cases
TF-IDF downweights common words and upweights distinctive words improving discrimination
TF-IDF converts words to their synonyms before counting improving vocabulary coverage
TF-IDF always produces fewer features than raw word counts making models train faster
explanation: TF-IDF downweights terms common across documents (like 'the') and upweights distinctive terms, creating more discriminative features than raw counts.

text: What is the purpose of creating lag features in time-series feature engineering?
options: They capture temporal dependencies by using past values as features for current predictions
Lag features convert time-series data into image format for convolutional neural networks
They eliminate seasonality from time-series data making it stationary for all models
Lag features remove temporal dependencies to make data suitable for standard ML models
explanation: Lag features use values from previous time steps as input features, allowing models to capture temporal patterns and autocorrelations in sequential data.

text: How does the permutation importance method evaluate feature relevance after model training?
options: It sorts features alphabetically and assigns importance based on their position order
It shuffles each feature's values and measures how much model performance degrades
It adds random noise to each feature and measures how much training accuracy improves
It removes each feature entirely and retrains the model from scratch to compare results
explanation: Permutation importance randomly shuffles each feature and measures performance degradation. Large drops indicate the feature is important for model predictions.

text: Why should you be cautious about using future information when engineering features for time-series models?
options: Using future values creates temporal leakage leading to unrealistically optimistic evaluation
Future information is always available at prediction time so there is no risk in using it
Future information always improves time-series model accuracy without any negative effects
Time-series models automatically detect and exclude future information during training
explanation: Using future values (like next month's average) creates temporal leakage - the model appears accurate in training but fails in production where future data is unavailable.

text: What is a neural network inspired by?
options: Database systems
Computer circuits
The human brain
Mathematical proofs
explanation: Neural networks are inspired by the structure and function of the human brain, with artificial neurons mimicking biological neurons.

text: What is an activation function in a neural network?
options: A function that loads training data
A function that saves the model
A function that introduces non-linearity
A function that cleans input data
explanation: Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks, enabling them to learn complex patterns beyond linear relationships.

text: What is a hidden layer in a neural network?
options: A layer between input and output
The output layer of the network
The first layer receiving raw data
The layer that stores data on disk
explanation: Hidden layers are the layers between the input and output layers where the neural network learns internal representations of the data.

text: Which activation function outputs values between 0 and 1?
options: Linear function
Tanh function
ReLU function
Sigmoid function
explanation: The sigmoid function squashes input values to the range (0, 1), making it useful for binary classification output layers.

text: What does the term 'epoch' mean in deep learning training?
options: The initialization of model weight values
One complete pass through all training data
A single forward pass of one sample
The final test evaluation of the model
explanation: An epoch is one complete pass through the entire training dataset during the model training process.

text: What is the purpose of backpropagation in neural networks?
options: To calculate and propagate error gradients
To make forward predictions on data
To load training data into memory
To visualize the network architecture
explanation: Backpropagation calculates error gradients by propagating them backward through the network, enabling weight updates via gradient descent.

text: What is a weight in a neural network?
options: The number of layers in the network
The learning rate of the optimizer
A learnable parameter connecting neurons
The size of the training dataset
explanation: Weights are learnable parameters that determine the strength of connections between neurons, adjusted during training to minimize error.

text: What is the most commonly used activation function in hidden layers today?
options: Sigmoid
Softmax
Linear
ReLU
explanation: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) is the most commonly used activation function in hidden layers due to its simplicity and effectiveness.

text: What does the output layer of a classification network produce?
options: Hidden representations
Class probability scores
Gradient computations
Raw pixel values
explanation: The output layer produces class probability scores, typically using softmax for multi-class or sigmoid for binary classification.

text: What is a bias term in a neural network?
options: The rate at which the model learns from training data
The preference of the model for certain training samples
The error in predictions caused by insufficient data size
An additional learnable parameter added to weighted sums
explanation: A bias is an additional learnable parameter added to the weighted sum of inputs, allowing the network to shift the activation function.

text: Why does the vanishing gradient problem occur in deep networks?
options: Because the learning rate is always set too high for deep architectures
The problem only occurs in shallow networks and never in deep architectures
Deep networks always have too few parameters to compute meaningful gradients
Gradients shrink exponentially through many layers making early layers learn slowly
explanation: With sigmoid/tanh activations, gradients are multiplied through many layers and shrink exponentially, making early layers learn extremely slowly.

text: How does batch normalization improve deep neural network training?
options: It normalizes layer inputs reducing internal covariate shift for faster training
It removes the need for any activation functions in the neural network layers
It replaces the optimizer and directly updates weights without gradient descent
It increases the batch size automatically to improve training speed for models
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes the inputs to each layer, reducing internal covariate shift, allowing higher learning rates and faster convergence.

text: What is the purpose of dropout regularization in neural networks?
options: It increases the number of neurons in each layer to improve model capacity
It drops entire training samples that the model already classifies correctly
It randomly deactivates neurons during training to prevent co-adaptation of features
It permanently removes neurons that have zero weight values in the network
explanation: Dropout randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during training, preventing co-adaptation and forcing the network to learn redundant representations.

text: How does the Adam optimizer combine momentum and adaptive learning rates?
options: Adam uses only momentum without any adaptive learning rate adjustment at all
It maintains running averages of both gradients and squared gradients for updates
It uses a fixed learning rate that never changes throughout the training process
Adam ignores gradient history and uses only the current gradient for each update
explanation: Adam maintains exponentially decaying averages of past gradients (momentum) and past squared gradients (adaptive rates), combining benefits of both approaches.

text: What is the difference between a dense layer and a convolutional layer?
options: Dense layers connect every neuron while convolutional layers use local receptive fields
Dense layers only work with images while convolutional layers work with tabular data
Dense layers use local connections while convolutional layers connect every neuron pair
Both layer types are identical and can be used interchangeably in any architecture
explanation: Dense layers connect every input to every output, while convolutional layers apply local filters (receptive fields) with shared weights across spatial positions.

text: Why is the learning rate one of the most important hyperparameters?
options: The learning rate only affects the final accuracy and not the training speed at all
It only matters for the first epoch and is automatically adjusted after that point
The learning rate has no effect on training dynamics and can be set to any value
Too high causes divergence and too low causes extremely slow convergence of loss
explanation: Learning rate controls update step size. Too high causes oscillation/divergence, too low causes extremely slow convergence. Finding the right value is critical.

text: What is transfer learning in the context of deep learning?
options: Transferring data from one storage system to another for model training use
Using a pre-trained network and fine-tuning it for a different related task
Training a model from random initialization on a completely new dataset only
Converting a classification model into a regression model without retraining
explanation: Transfer learning uses a pre-trained network (often on ImageNet) and fine-tunes it for a new task, leveraging learned representations to reduce data and training needs.

text: How does gradient clipping prevent training instability in deep networks?
options: It caps gradient values at a maximum threshold preventing exploding gradients
It removes all gradients entirely preventing any weight updates from occurring
It increases gradient values when they are too small to accelerate convergence
It replaces all negative gradients with zero to ensure only positive updates
explanation: Gradient clipping caps gradient values at a threshold, preventing exploding gradients that cause training instability and divergence in deep networks.

text: What is the purpose of an embedding layer in deep learning?
options: To reduce the learning rate during training to prevent overfitting of model
To map discrete tokens to dense continuous vector representations efficiently
To normalize the input data before it enters the first hidden layer values
To compute the final loss value at the output of the neural network model
explanation: Embedding layers map discrete items (words, categories) to dense continuous vectors, capturing semantic relationships in a lower-dimensional space.

text: Why do deep learning models typically require GPU acceleration?
options: GPUs have more storage space than CPUs for holding the training dataset files
GPUs excel at parallel matrix operations which dominate deep learning computation
GPUs are required by all deep learning frameworks and cannot use CPU at all
GPUs automatically optimize hyperparameters while CPUs cannot perform this task
explanation: Deep learning involves massive matrix multiplications that GPUs parallelize efficiently, providing 10-100x speedup over CPUs for training.

text: Why do residual connections in ResNet help train very deep networks effectively?
options: They allow gradients to flow directly through skip connections preventing vanishing
Residual connections double the number of parameters in each layer of network
Residual connections remove the need for any activation functions in the network
They automatically reduce the learning rate as the network depth increases here
explanation: Skip connections in ResNet provide a direct gradient path, allowing gradients to bypass layers and preventing vanishing gradients in very deep networks.

text: What is the dying ReLU problem and how is Leaky ReLU a solution?
options: Dying ReLU only affects the output layer while Leaky ReLU only affects hidden layers
ReLU never has this problem and Leaky ReLU was created for a different purpose
Dying ReLU means the function grows too large while Leaky ReLU caps the output
Neurons with negative inputs permanently output zero and Leaky ReLU allows small negatives
explanation: When ReLU inputs are consistently negative, neurons output zero permanently (die). Leaky ReLU allows a small negative slope, keeping neurons alive and learnable.

text: How does the attention mechanism in neural networks improve sequence modeling?
options: It forces the model to process all elements equally regardless of their relevance
It removes the need for any training data by using pre-computed attention weights
It limits the model to processing only the first and last elements of sequences
It allows the model to dynamically focus on relevant parts of the input sequence
explanation: Attention computes weighted sums of input elements based on their relevance to the current context, allowing models to focus on the most important parts dynamically.

text: Why is the choice of weight initialization strategy critical for deep network training?
options: Weight initialization has no effect on training since optimizers always find the same solution
Weight initialization only matters for the first layer and has no effect on deeper layers
Poor initialization can cause vanishing or exploding activations preventing effective learning
All initialization strategies produce identical results after sufficient training epochs pass
explanation: Poor initialization (too large or too small) causes activations to explode or vanish through layers. Strategies like Xavier and He initialization maintain appropriate activation scales.

text: What is the lottery ticket hypothesis and its implications for neural network pruning?
options: It suggests that random network architectures always outperform carefully designed ones
It proves that larger networks always outperform smaller networks without any exceptions
Dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that achieve comparable accuracy when trained alone
It states that all neural network weights are equally important and none can be removed
explanation: The lottery ticket hypothesis states that dense networks contain sparse subnetworks (winning tickets) that can match full network performance when trained independently.

text: How does knowledge distillation compress a large model into a smaller one?
options: It copies all weights directly from the large model into the smaller model architecture
It trains the small model only on data that the large model classified incorrectly
A smaller student model learns to mimic the soft output probabilities of a larger teacher
It removes random layers from the large model until the desired size is reached
explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to match the soft probability outputs of a larger teacher model, transferring learned knowledge into a compact architecture.

text: Why do neural architecture search methods face a massive computational challenge?
options: The challenge is purely theoretical and modern hardware makes NAS trivially inexpensive
NAS only searches over learning rates not architectures so the space is very small
The search space of possible architectures is exponentially large requiring many evaluations
Neural architecture search always converges in a single iteration without much computation
explanation: The search space of possible architectures (layers, connections, operations) is exponentially large, and each candidate requires training to evaluate, making exhaustive search infeasible.

text: What role does the temperature parameter play in softmax for knowledge distillation?
options: Temperature only affects training speed and has no effect on the output distribution
Temperature is fixed at one and cannot be changed in any softmax implementation ever
Lower temperature always produces better distillation results regardless of the task
Higher temperature produces softer probabilities revealing more about learned similarities
explanation: Higher temperature softens the probability distribution, revealing inter-class similarities that hard labels lack, which provides richer information for the student model.

text: How does mixed precision training accelerate deep learning while maintaining accuracy?
options: It halves the training data to reduce computation time at the cost of some accuracy
It skips every other layer during forward pass to achieve faster computation speed
It uses float16 for most operations and float32 for critical ones reducing memory and time
It uses only 8-bit integers for all computations throughout the entire training process
explanation: Mixed precision uses float16 for most computations (faster, less memory) while maintaining float32 for loss scaling and critical accumulations to preserve training stability.

text: What is the neural tangent kernel framework and why is it theoretically important?
options: It is a specific type of convolutional kernel used only for image recognition tasks
It is a pruning technique for removing unnecessary connections from trained networks
It is an optimizer that replaces gradient descent for all neural network training
It shows infinite-width networks behave like kernel methods enabling theoretical analysis
explanation: The NTK framework shows that infinitely wide neural networks behave like kernel machines during training, enabling theoretical analysis of convergence and generalization.

text: What does CNN stand for in deep learning?
options: Central Neural Network
Circular Neuron Network
Convolutional Neural Network
Connected Node Network
explanation: CNN stands for Convolutional Neural Network, a type of neural network designed primarily for processing grid-like data such as images.

text: What does RNN stand for in deep learning?
options: Recursive Node Network
Recurrent Neural Network
Regional Neuron Network
Random Neural Network
explanation: RNN stands for Recurrent Neural Network, designed to process sequential data by maintaining a hidden state across time steps.

text: What is a convolutional filter used for in CNNs?
options: Storing training labels
Detecting patterns in input
Compressing the model size
Removing noise from data
explanation: Convolutional filters slide across input data to detect patterns like edges, textures, and shapes at different spatial locations.

text: What is the purpose of a pooling layer in a CNN?
options: To add more features to data
To increase image resolution
To reduce spatial dimensions
To convert images to text
explanation: Pooling layers reduce the spatial dimensions of feature maps, decreasing computational cost and providing translation invariance.

text: What type of data are RNNs primarily designed to handle?
options: Tabular data
Image data
Graph data
Sequential data
explanation: RNNs are designed for sequential data like time series, text, and speech, where the order of elements matters.

text: What is a GAN in deep learning?
options: Grouped Attention Network
Gradient Adjusted Network
General Analysis Network
Generative Adversarial Network
explanation: A GAN (Generative Adversarial Network) consists of a generator and discriminator that compete to produce realistic synthetic data.

text: What is max pooling in a CNN?
options: Taking the average value from a region
Taking the median value from a region
Taking the maximum value from a region
Taking the minimum value from a region
explanation: Max pooling selects the maximum value from each region of the feature map, retaining the most prominent features.

text: What is an autoencoder used for?
options: Generating adversarial examples
Learning compressed data representations
Tuning model hyperparameters
Supervised classification tasks
explanation: An autoencoder learns compressed representations of data by training to reconstruct its input through a bottleneck layer.

text: What does LSTM stand for?
options: Layered Spatial Tensor Model
Local Statistical Testing Method
Long Short-Term Memory
Linear Sequential Training Model
explanation: LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory, a type of RNN designed to learn long-range dependencies in sequential data.

text: What is the Transformer architecture primarily known for?
options: Reinforcement learning games
Image classification tasks
Self-attention mechanism for sequences
Database query optimization
explanation: The Transformer architecture is known for its self-attention mechanism, enabling efficient parallel processing of sequences.

text: How does the LSTM cell solve the vanishing gradient problem of standard RNNs?
options: It processes sequences in reverse order to reduce the effective sequence length
It uses a much larger learning rate than standard RNN implementations typically use
It uses gates to control information flow allowing gradients to persist long-term
It removes all activation functions to ensure gradients never shrink in magnitude
explanation: LSTM uses input, forget, and output gates that control information flow through a cell state, allowing gradients to flow across many time steps without vanishing.

text: What is the purpose of the discriminator in a GAN?
options: To distinguish between real data samples and generated fake data samples
To preprocess input data before it is fed to the generator network model
To generate realistic synthetic data samples from random noise input vectors
To evaluate the final quality of the trained model on a held-out test set
explanation: The discriminator learns to distinguish real data from fake data produced by the generator, providing the adversarial signal that drives the generator to improve.

text: How does dilated convolution differ from standard convolution in CNNs?
options: Dilated convolution uses smaller filters than standard convolution always does
Standard convolution has larger receptive fields than dilated convolution does
Dilated convolution inserts gaps between filter elements expanding receptive field
Dilated convolution only works with one-dimensional input data like text only
explanation: Dilated (atrous) convolution inserts spaces between filter elements, expanding the receptive field without increasing parameters or reducing resolution.

text: What is the key innovation of the self-attention mechanism in Transformers?
options: It eliminates the need for any training data by using pre-defined attention maps
It requires data to be processed in strict sequential order one step at a time
It only attends to the immediately preceding element in the input sequence
It computes relationships between all positions in a sequence simultaneously
explanation: Self-attention computes pairwise relationships between all positions simultaneously, enabling parallelism and capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence.

text: What problem does mode collapse cause in GAN training?
options: The discriminator becomes too weak to provide any useful training signal at all
The generator produces diverse outputs but none of them look like real data
The generator produces realistic but limited variety of outputs ignoring modes
The training process converges too slowly requiring excessive computation time
explanation: Mode collapse occurs when the generator learns to produce only a few realistic outputs, ignoring the full diversity of the real data distribution.

text: How do 1x1 convolutions serve as a dimensionality reduction technique in deep networks?
options: They only work with one-dimensional data and cannot be applied to image features
They remove the need for any pooling layers in the convolutional neural network
They increase the spatial dimensions of the feature maps while reducing channels
They reduce the number of channels by combining feature maps with learned weights
explanation: 1x1 convolutions combine channels using learned weights, reducing the channel dimension while preserving spatial dimensions, used in architectures like Inception.

text: What is the purpose of positional encoding in Transformer architectures?
options: It replaces the need for any attention mechanism in the Transformer model
It determines the optimal position for inserting new layers during training
It encodes the position of the model parameters in memory for faster access
It injects sequence position information since self-attention has no order notion
explanation: Since self-attention is permutation-invariant, positional encoding adds position information so the model can distinguish element ordering in sequences.

text: How does depthwise separable convolution reduce computational cost?
options: It removes all bias terms from the convolutional layers to reduce parameters
It skips every other spatial position during convolution to halve computation
It uses only the first channel of input and ignores all other channels entirely
It separates spatial and channel-wise filtering reducing multiply-add operations
explanation: Depthwise separable convolution factorizes standard convolution into depthwise (spatial) and pointwise (channel) operations, dramatically reducing computation.

text: What is the role of the encoder and decoder in a sequence-to-sequence model?
options: The encoder handles training while the decoder is only used during evaluation
The encoder generates output and decoder processes the raw input sequence data
Both encoder and decoder perform identical operations on the same input data set
The encoder compresses input to a representation and decoder generates the output
explanation: The encoder processes the input sequence into a context representation, and the decoder generates the output sequence from that representation.

text: What advantage does the GRU have over the standard LSTM architecture?
options: GRU uses fewer gates and parameters achieving similar performance more efficiently
GRU can process images while LSTM is limited to only text and audio data types
GRU does not use any gating mechanism unlike LSTM which uses three gates total
GRU always achieves higher accuracy than LSTM on every sequential task given
explanation: GRU combines the forget and input gates into a single update gate and merges cell and hidden states, using fewer parameters while achieving comparable performance.

text: Why do Vision Transformers require large-scale pre-training to outperform CNNs?
options: Vision Transformers have fewer parameters than CNNs and need more data to compensate
CNNs always outperform Vision Transformers regardless of the training data scale used
Vision Transformers can only process text data and need pre-training to handle images
ViTs lack inductive biases like locality and translation equivariance that CNNs have
explanation: ViTs lack CNN's inductive biases (locality, translation equivariance), so they need large-scale data to learn these spatial relationships from scratch.

text: How does the Wasserstein loss improve GAN training stability compared to the original formulation?
options: It eliminates the need for a discriminator network in the adversarial training setup
It provides smoother gradients based on earth mover distance even when distributions differ
Wasserstein loss only works with image generation and not with other data modalities
It uses the same loss as the original GAN but with a different optimizer algorithm
explanation: Wasserstein loss uses earth mover's distance, providing meaningful gradients even when real and generated distributions don't overlap, unlike JS divergence which can saturate.

text: What is the computational complexity of self-attention and how do efficient variants address it?
options: Efficient variants make attention slower but produce more accurate attention weights
Self-attention is O(n) and efficient variants increase it to O(n squared) for accuracy
Self-attention is O(n squared) and variants like Linformer use projections for O(n)
Self-attention has constant complexity regardless of the sequence length provided
explanation: Standard self-attention is O(n^2) in sequence length. Efficient variants (Linformer, Performer) use projections or kernelization to achieve linear O(n) complexity.

text: How does the diffusion model generate high-quality images compared to GANs?
options: They require significantly less training data than GANs to produce comparable results
Diffusion models train adversarially like GANs but with more discriminator networks
Diffusion models generate images in a single forward pass without any iterative steps
They learn to reverse a gradual noising process producing diverse high-quality samples
explanation: Diffusion models learn to reverse a gradual noise addition process, iteratively denoising to generate samples. This avoids mode collapse and training instability common in GANs.

text: What is the significance of the universal approximation theorem for neural networks?
options: It guarantees that deeper networks always outperform shallower networks on all tasks
A sufficiently wide single-layer network can approximate any continuous function closely
It states that neural networks can only approximate linear functions and nothing else
It proves that neural networks always find the global optimum during training runs
explanation: The universal approximation theorem states a single hidden layer network with enough neurons can approximate any continuous function, though it doesn't guarantee learnability.

text: How does neural architecture search with weight sharing reduce computational cost?
options: It trains each candidate architecture from scratch independently on separate hardware
It searches only over learning rates not architectures making the space much smaller
Multiple architectures share weights from a supernet avoiding full retraining each time
Weight sharing eliminates the need for any gradient computation during architecture search
explanation: Weight sharing (one-shot NAS) trains a supernet containing all candidate architectures, allowing each to be evaluated using shared weights without training from scratch.

text: Why is training stability a major challenge in GAN optimization?
options: GAN training is always stable and converges to the optimal solution every single time
The minimax game between generator and discriminator can oscillate without convergence
The generator and discriminator always converge at the exact same rate making it easy
Stability issues only occur with very small datasets of fewer than hundred total samples
explanation: The minimax optimization between generator and discriminator is inherently unstable - if one dominates, the other cannot learn, leading to oscillation or mode collapse.

text: How does the mixture of experts architecture achieve computational efficiency in large models?
options: It reduces model size by removing most parameters from each expert subnetwork
It uses a single large expert for all inputs and ignores the routing mechanism
It routes each input to only a subset of experts reducing computation per sample
It trains all expert networks on every input sample to ensure comprehensive coverage
explanation: MoE routes each input to a small subset of specialized expert networks via a gating mechanism, achieving large model capacity with sparse computation per sample.

text: What is the role of contrastive loss in self-supervised representation learning?
options: It replaces all other loss functions and is the only one used in deep learning
It only works with generative models and cannot be used with discriminative ones
It pulls similar sample representations together and pushes dissimilar ones apart
It requires labeled data pairs to compute the loss function during training phase
explanation: Contrastive loss learns representations by pulling positive pairs (augmented views) together and pushing negative pairs apart in embedding space.

text: How does the Flash Attention algorithm improve Transformer training efficiency?
options: It uses tiling and recomputation to reduce memory IO making attention faster on GPU
Flash Attention only works on CPU hardware and provides no benefit on GPU systems
It reduces attention accuracy to save computation time during the training process
It removes the attention mechanism entirely replacing it with simple averaging only
explanation: Flash Attention uses IO-aware tiling to compute attention without materializing the full attention matrix in HBM, reducing memory reads/writes for significant speedups.

text: What does NLP stand for?
options: Neural Logic Processing
Numerical Linear Programming
Natural Language Processing
Network Layer Protocol
explanation: NLP stands for Natural Language Processing, a field of AI focused on enabling computers to understand and process human language.

text: What is tokenization in NLP?
options: Encrypting text for security use
Compressing text to save space
Splitting text into smaller units
Translating text between languages
explanation: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units (tokens) such as words, subwords, or characters for processing.

text: What is sentiment analysis used for?
options: Detecting the emotion or opinion in text
Counting the words in a document
Correcting grammar errors in writing
Translating text to other languages
explanation: Sentiment analysis determines the emotional tone or opinion expressed in text, classifying it as positive, negative, or neutral.

text: What is a stop word in natural language processing?
options: A common word often removed in preprocessing
A word that stops program execution
A word that contains a typing error
A word used only in technical documents
explanation: Stop words are common words like 'the', 'is', 'at' that are often removed during text preprocessing because they carry little meaning.

text: What is named entity recognition?
options: Identifying names of programming functions in code
Identifying proper nouns like people and places in text
Identifying the most frequent word in a paragraph text
Identifying syntax errors in written documents for fixes
explanation: Named Entity Recognition (NER) identifies and classifies proper nouns in text into categories like persons, organizations, and locations.

text: What is stemming in text preprocessing?
options: Reducing words to their root or base form
Sorting words in alphabetical sequence
Adding prefixes to expand word meanings
Translating words to another language
explanation: Stemming reduces words to their root form by removing suffixes, e.g., 'running' becomes 'run', simplifying text analysis.

text: What is a corpus in NLP?
options: A neural network architecture
A large collection of text data
A visualization chart format
A machine learning algorithm type
explanation: A corpus is a large, structured collection of text documents used for training and evaluating NLP models.

text: What does a language model predict?
options: The next word in a sequence
Hardware specifications
Database query results
Image classification labels
explanation: A language model predicts the probability of the next word in a sequence based on the preceding context.

text: What is text classification in NLP?
options: Translating text between two languages
Converting speech to written text
Generating images from descriptions
Assigning categories to text documents
explanation: Text classification assigns predefined categories or labels to text documents, such as spam detection or topic categorization.

text: What is a word embedding?
options: A word encrypted for secure storage
A word stored in a database table
A word highlighted in a text editor
A dense vector representation of a word
explanation: Word embeddings are dense vector representations that capture semantic meaning, placing similar words closer together in vector space.

text: How does TF-IDF improve upon simple bag-of-words representation?
options: It counts words more quickly than bag-of-words without changing the representation
It weights words by importance reducing the influence of common uninformative terms
It only considers the first and last words in each document ignoring all others
TF-IDF and bag-of-words produce identical representations for all text documents
explanation: TF-IDF weights terms by frequency in the document (TF) and rarity across the corpus (IDF), reducing the influence of common words like 'the'.

text: What is the key innovation of Word2Vec compared to one-hot encoding?
options: Word2Vec only works with very small vocabularies of less than one hundred words total
Word2Vec produces identical representations as one-hot encoding for all vocabulary items
Word2Vec requires labeled data for every word pair relationship in the corpus used
Word2Vec creates dense vectors capturing semantic relationships between words efficiently
explanation: Word2Vec learns dense vector representations where semantically similar words have similar vectors, unlike sparse one-hot encoding that captures no semantic relationships.

text: What is the difference between stemming and lemmatization?
options: Stemming applies crude rules while lemmatization uses vocabulary and morphological analysis
Stemming uses dictionary-based root finding while lemmatization uses rule-based cutting
Stemming is more accurate than lemmatization for all natural language processing tasks
Both methods always produce identical output for every word in all languages used
explanation: Stemming applies crude heuristic rules (chopping suffixes), while lemmatization uses vocabulary and morphological analysis to find proper root forms.

text: How does the attention mechanism in seq2seq models improve machine translation?
options: It allows the decoder to focus on relevant source words for each output word generated
Attention removes the need for both encoder and decoder in the translation pipeline
It forces the decoder to translate each word independently without any context used
It limits the model to translating only between English and one other target language
explanation: Attention allows the decoder to dynamically focus on the most relevant source words when generating each target word, handling long sentences better than fixed context vectors.

text: What is the purpose of byte-pair encoding in modern NLP tokenizers?
options: It encrypts text using byte-level operations for secure communication purposes
It splits every word into individual characters without any merging of subwords
It converts all text to lowercase and removes all punctuation marks from strings
It iteratively merges frequent character pairs creating a subword vocabulary set
explanation: BPE iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs, creating subword units that balance vocabulary size with the ability to represent any word.

text: How does the BERT model differ from earlier language models in its training approach?
options: BERT uses bidirectional context by masking random tokens and predicting them
BERT uses left-to-right unidirectional context like all previous language models did
BERT processes text character by character unlike word-level previous models
BERT requires no pre-training and is trained only on downstream task data sets
explanation: BERT uses masked language modeling, randomly masking tokens and predicting them using bidirectional context, unlike earlier left-to-right unidirectional models.

text: What is the purpose of the CLS token in BERT?
options: It separates individual characters within each word of the input sequence
It indicates missing or unknown words in the vocabulary of the tokenizer
It provides a pooled representation for classification tasks using the model
It marks the end of every sentence in the input text sequence provided
explanation: The [CLS] token's final hidden state serves as an aggregate representation of the entire input, used for classification tasks downstream.

text: What is perplexity and how is it used to evaluate language models?
options: It measures the vocabulary size of the language model's training corpus data
It measures the training time required to converge to the optimal solution found
It counts the number of parameters in the language model architecture structure
It measures how well the model predicts text with lower values being better scores
explanation: Perplexity measures how surprised the model is by test text, with lower perplexity indicating better prediction. It equals the exponentiation of the average cross-entropy loss.

text: How does transfer learning with pre-trained models benefit NLP tasks with limited data?
options: Transfer learning only works when the new task has more data than the pre-training set
Pre-trained models must always be retrained from scratch on new task specific data
Pre-trained models provide rich language understanding that transfers to new tasks well
Pre-trained models can only be used for the exact task they were originally trained on
explanation: Pre-trained models learn general language understanding from large corpora, which transfers to downstream tasks, enabling good performance even with limited task-specific data.

text: What is the difference between extractive and abstractive text summarization?
options: Extractive selects existing sentences while abstractive generates new sentences from scratch
Extractive only works with short texts while abstractive only works with long documents
Both methods always produce identical summaries for any given input document provided
Extractive generates new sentences while abstractive selects existing sentences from input
explanation: Extractive summarization selects and combines existing sentences, while abstractive generates new sentences that may not appear in the original text.

text: Why do large language models sometimes generate factually incorrect but plausible-sounding text?
options: LLMs verify all facts against a database before generating any output text content
LLMs always generate perfectly accurate text since they are trained on factual data
Factual errors only occur when the model has fewer than one million total parameters
LLMs learn statistical patterns rather than factual knowledge causing hallucination errors
explanation: LLMs learn statistical patterns in text rather than grounded factual knowledge, so they can generate fluent text that is factually wrong (hallucination) because it matches learned patterns.

text: How does the Transformer's multi-head attention provide an advantage over single-head attention?
options: Multi-head attention is identical to single-head attention and provides no real advantage
Multiple heads only work with image data and provide no benefit for text processing
Multiple heads attend to different representation subspaces capturing diverse relationships
Multi-head attention uses fewer parameters than single-head attention in all cases
explanation: Multiple attention heads operate in different representation subspaces simultaneously, each potentially capturing different types of relationships (syntactic, semantic, positional).

text: What is the fundamental challenge of evaluating open-ended text generation models?
options: Open-ended generation models always produce identical outputs for the same input text
Evaluation is trivial because BLEU score perfectly captures all aspects of text quality
There is always exactly one correct output for any given text generation prompt used
Multiple valid outputs exist making automatic metrics insufficient for quality assessment
explanation: Open-ended generation has many valid outputs, making automatic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) insufficient. They correlate poorly with human judgments of fluency, coherence, and creativity.

text: How does reinforcement learning from human feedback improve language model alignment?
options: RLHF only affects the tokenizer and has no impact on the language model weights
It fine-tunes the model using a reward signal trained on human preference judgments
It replaces the Transformer architecture with a recurrent neural network approach
RLHF removes all pre-training knowledge and trains the model entirely from scratch
explanation: RLHF trains a reward model on human preferences, then uses it as a reward signal to fine-tune the LM via reinforcement learning, aligning outputs with human values.

text: Why is the position of negation words critical for sentiment analysis accuracy?
options: Negation words have no effect on sentiment and can be safely removed during preprocessing
Negation reverses sentiment of subsequent words and mishandling it causes incorrect polarity
Negation only appears in formal text and never occurs in informal social media content
All negation words are stop words and are automatically removed by all NLP pipelines used
explanation: Negation words (not, never, hardly) reverse the sentiment of subsequent words. Failing to properly handle negation scope causes sentiment classifiers to predict incorrect polarity.

text: What is the catastrophic forgetting problem when fine-tuning pre-trained language models?
options: Catastrophic forgetting only occurs in computer vision models and never in NLP models
The model loses general pre-trained knowledge when heavily adapted to a specific new task
Fine-tuning always improves performance on all tasks simultaneously without any tradeoffs
Fine-tuning adds new knowledge without affecting any previously learned representations
explanation: Heavy fine-tuning on a specific task can overwrite the general language understanding learned during pre-training, degrading performance on other tasks.

text: How do retrieval-augmented generation models reduce hallucination in language models?
options: They remove the generative component entirely and only return retrieved document text
They retrieve relevant documents and condition generation on factual source material
RAG models eliminate hallucination completely with a one hundred percent success rate
They increase the model size to store more factual information in the network parameters
explanation: RAG combines retrieval of relevant documents with generation, grounding the model's output in actual source material to reduce fabrication of facts.

text: Why is cross-lingual transfer learning effective for low-resource languages?
options: Multilingual models learn shared representations that transfer knowledge between languages
Cross-lingual transfer degrades performance on all languages including high-resource ones
Each language requires a completely separate model with no knowledge sharing possible
Cross-lingual transfer only works between languages that share the same script system
explanation: Multilingual models like mBERT learn shared cross-lingual representations, allowing knowledge from high-resource languages to improve performance on low-resource languages.

text: What is the computational challenge of scaling attention to very long document contexts?
options: Long documents always produce better results so there is no computational challenge here
Long documents can be processed in constant time regardless of their total length overall
Attention memory and compute scale quadratically with sequence length becoming prohibitive
Attention complexity is independent of sequence length and depends only on model depth
explanation: Standard attention requires O(n^2) memory and compute for sequence length n, making it prohibitive for long documents (e.g., 100K tokens), motivating efficient attention variants.

text: How does chain-of-thought prompting improve reasoning in large language models?
options: It breaks complex problems into intermediate steps allowing systematic reasoning output
It reduces the model's accuracy but makes the output more readable for human reviewers
Chain-of-thought only works for arithmetic and cannot improve any other reasoning tasks
It forces the model to generate the answer in a single token without any explanation
explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting elicits step-by-step reasoning, helping LLMs decompose complex problems into manageable intermediate steps, improving accuracy on reasoning tasks.

text: What is computer vision?
options: A computer's screen resolution settings
AI field enabling machines to interpret images
A method for compressing video file data
A type of display monitor hardware
explanation: Computer vision is an AI field that enables machines to interpret and understand visual information from images and videos.

text: What is image classification?
options: Converting images to text format
Sorting images by file size on disk
Assigning a label to an entire image
Splitting an image into two halves
explanation: Image classification assigns a categorical label to an entire image, identifying what the image contains.

text: What does object detection identify in an image?
options: The resolution of the image file
The file format of the image data
The color profile of the image data
Objects and their bounding box locations
explanation: Object detection identifies objects in an image and provides their locations using bounding boxes around each detected object.

text: What is a pixel in digital image processing?
options: The smallest unit of a digital image
A color space conversion algorithm
A type of image compression format
A filter used to sharpen images
explanation: A pixel (picture element) is the smallest addressable unit of a digital image, containing color and intensity information.

text: What does image segmentation do?
options: Converts color images to grayscale
Increases image resolution quality
Divides image into meaningful regions
Reduces the file size of images
explanation: Image segmentation divides an image into meaningful regions or segments, assigning each pixel to a category or object.

text: What is data augmentation in computer vision?
options: Reducing the resolution of all images
Converting all images to black and white
Deleting duplicate images from dataset
Creating modified copies of training images
explanation: Data augmentation creates modified copies of training images (rotations, flips, crops) to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.

text: Which deep learning architecture is most associated with image processing?
options: Long Short-Term Memory
Generative Adversarial Network
Recurrent Neural Network
Convolutional Neural Network
explanation: CNNs are the most widely used architecture for image processing, using convolutional filters to detect spatial patterns.

text: What is face recognition technology used for?
options: Converting faces into cartoon drawings
Identifying or verifying a person's identity
Compressing facial images to save space
Measuring the resolution of face images
explanation: Face recognition technology identifies or verifies a person's identity by analyzing facial features in images or video.

text: What is optical character recognition?
options: Recognizing and extracting text from images
Compressing text before saving to disk
Converting text files to images
Encrypting text within image files
explanation: OCR (Optical Character Recognition) recognizes and extracts text from images, scanned documents, or photographs of text.

text: What does RGB stand for in digital images?
options: Random Generated Bits
Red Green Blue
Regular Grid Base
Rapid Graphics Buffer
explanation: RGB stands for Red, Green, Blue - the three primary color channels used to represent colors in digital images.

text: How does transfer learning with pre-trained CNNs benefit computer vision tasks with limited data?
options: Transfer learning only works for text classification and not for any image-based tasks
Early layers learn general features that transfer well reducing data needs for new tasks
Pre-trained models always perform worse than randomly initialized models on new tasks
Pre-trained models must be completely retrained from scratch for every new CV task
explanation: Early CNN layers learn general features (edges, textures) transferable across tasks. Fine-tuning only later layers requires much less task-specific data.

text: What is the difference between semantic and instance segmentation?
options: Semantic segmentation only works on grayscale images while instance works on color
Semantic labels each pixel by class while instance distinguishes individual objects too
Both segmentation types always produce identical pixel-level output maps on images
Instance labels each pixel by class while semantic distinguishes individual objects only
explanation: Semantic segmentation labels every pixel with a class but doesn't distinguish between different instances. Instance segmentation identifies each individual object separately.

text: How does the YOLO architecture achieve real-time object detection?
options: It only detects objects in the center of the image ignoring all border regions always
It frames detection as a single regression problem processing the entire image at once
It processes each potential object region separately using a sliding window approach
YOLO requires multiple passes through the image increasing detection time significantly
explanation: YOLO (You Only Look Once) treats detection as a single regression problem, dividing the image into a grid and predicting boxes and classes in one forward pass.

text: What is the purpose of non-maximum suppression in object detection?
options: To maximize the size of all bounding boxes to ensure objects are fully contained
To increase the number of detected bounding boxes for better detection coverage
To remove redundant overlapping bounding boxes keeping only the best predictions
To suppress all detections with confidence scores above a certain threshold level
explanation: NMS removes redundant overlapping bounding boxes, keeping only the one with the highest confidence for each detected object.

text: How does image augmentation help prevent overfitting in computer vision models?
options: It creates diverse variations forcing the model to learn invariant features robustly
It reduces the training set size to prevent the model from memorizing all samples
