Augmentation always causes more overfitting because it adds artificial noise to data
It only works with very large datasets and has no benefit for small training sets
explanation: Augmentation creates variations (rotations, flips, color jitter) that force the model to learn features invariant to these transformations, improving generalization.

text: What is the Intersection over Union metric used for in object detection?
options: Measuring the total number of pixels in the input image being processed by model
Determining the optimal learning rate for the detection model training run
Measuring the overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding box regions
Calculating the training time for the object detection model on GPU hardware
explanation: IoU measures the overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, computed as their intersection area divided by their union area.

text: How do feature pyramid networks improve multi-scale object detection?
options: They only detect objects at a single fixed scale ignoring all other size variations
They reduce all objects to the same size before detection to simplify the algorithm
They build a pyramid of feature maps at different scales for detecting objects of various sizes
Feature pyramids increase detection time without improving accuracy at any scale value
explanation: FPNs build top-down feature pyramids with lateral connections, creating rich features at all scales for detecting objects of varying sizes.

text: What is the role of anchor boxes in modern object detection architectures?
options: Anchor boxes are only used during evaluation and have no effect during training
They provide predefined reference boxes of various sizes and ratios for detection
They replace the non-maximum suppression step with a simpler averaging method
They fix the position of all detected objects to the center of the input image always
explanation: Anchor boxes are predefined reference boxes with various aspect ratios and scales at each spatial location, serving as starting points for bounding box regression.

text: How does a U-Net architecture achieve precise segmentation of medical images?
options: It uses skip connections between encoder and decoder to preserve spatial details
U-Net only works with natural images and cannot process medical imaging data well
It requires pixel-level annotations for only ten percent of the training images used
It only uses downsampling layers without any upsampling to produce segmentation maps
explanation: U-Net's skip connections pass high-resolution features from the encoder to the decoder, preserving spatial details crucial for precise segmentation boundaries.

text: What is the purpose of batch normalization in deep CNN architectures?
options: It normalizes layer activations stabilizing training and allowing higher learning rates
Batch normalization increases training time without providing any accuracy benefit
It only works with fully connected layers and cannot be used in convolutional layers
It removes all color information from input images before processing them further
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes activations within each mini-batch, stabilizing gradient flow, enabling higher learning rates, and acting as a mild regularizer.

text: Why do adversarial examples pose a serious threat to deployed computer vision systems?
options: Adversarial examples only affect models during training and never at inference time
All modern CNN architectures are completely immune to any adversarial perturbations
Adversarial examples only exist in theory and cannot be generated for real images
Imperceptible perturbations can cause confident misclassification in production models
explanation: Small, imperceptible perturbations can cause CNNs to make confident incorrect predictions, posing serious risks for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving.

text: How does contrastive learning for visual representations reduce dependence on labeled data?
options: It completely replaces supervised fine-tuning and achieves perfect accuracy on all tasks
Contrastive learning only works with text data and cannot be applied to images ever
It requires more labeled data than supervised learning to achieve comparable accuracy
It learns representations by contrasting augmented views of images without any labels
explanation: Contrastive learning creates positive pairs from augmented views of the same image and pushes different images apart, learning useful visual representations without labels.

text: What is the domain adaptation challenge when deploying CV models across different environments?
options: Distribution differences between training and deployment environments degrade performance
Domain adaptation is only relevant for NLP tasks and does not apply to computer vision
All visual environments produce identical image statistics so adaptation is unnecessary
Models trained in one visual domain always generalize perfectly to all other domains
explanation: Visual domain shift (different lighting, camera angles, weather) between training and deployment environments causes performance degradation, requiring domain adaptation techniques.

text: How does the panoptic segmentation task unify semantic and instance segmentation?
options: It assigns every pixel both a semantic class label and an instance identifier value
It replaces both tasks with a simpler bounding box detection approach for efficiency
Panoptic segmentation only works on outdoor scenes and cannot process indoor images
It only performs semantic segmentation and completely ignores instance-level information
explanation: Panoptic segmentation assigns every pixel a semantic class and an instance ID (for countable objects), providing a complete scene understanding combining both tasks.

text: Why is 3D object detection from point clouds fundamentally different from 2D image detection?
options: Point clouds are identical to images and can be processed by standard 2D CNN methods
3D detection is always easier than 2D detection because more information is available
Point clouds are sparse, unordered, and require specialized architectures like PointNet
Point clouds can only represent indoor environments and not outdoor driving scenarios
explanation: Point clouds are sparse, unordered, and irregularly sampled unlike dense regular images. Specialized architectures like PointNet directly process raw points respecting these properties.

text: What is the role of the feature extractor backbone in modern object detection frameworks?
options: It handles data augmentation and preprocessing before the actual detection begins
It generates the final bounding box predictions and class labels for each object found
The backbone is only used during training and is removed completely during inference
It extracts hierarchical visual features that the detection head uses for predictions
explanation: The backbone (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet) extracts rich hierarchical features from the input image, which the detection head then uses to predict bounding boxes and classes.

text: How does self-supervised pre-training with masked image modeling work for vision models?
options: It only works with text-image pairs and cannot be applied to images alone at all
It masks random image patches and trains the model to reconstruct the missing content
It requires fully labeled segmentation maps for every image in the training dataset
It trains models to classify images into predefined categories using labeled datasets
explanation: Masked image modeling (like MAE) randomly masks patches of images and trains the model to reconstruct them, learning rich visual representations without labels.

text: Why is temporal consistency important in video object detection and how is it maintained?
options: Temporal consistency is irrelevant because each video frame should be processed independently
Video frames are always identical so temporal consistency is automatically guaranteed here
Flickering predictions between frames indicate instability and tracking methods enforce consistency
Temporal consistency only matters for video compression and has no effect on detection
explanation: Without temporal consistency, detections flicker between frames. Tracking methods, temporal attention, and flow-based feature propagation maintain stable predictions across video frames.

text: What is the advantage of vision-language models like CLIP for zero-shot image classification?
options: They require separate fine-tuning for every possible class label before making predictions
Vision-language models can only classify images into the exact categories seen during training
They always perform worse than supervised models regardless of the number of training classes
They match images to text descriptions enabling classification without task-specific training
explanation: CLIP learns to match images with text descriptions in a shared embedding space, enabling classification of arbitrary categories described in natural language without task-specific training.

text: How does neural radiance field technology enable novel view synthesis from images?
options: It requires thousands of input images from every possible angle of the target scene
It simply stitches existing images together without any three-dimensional understanding
It learns a volumetric scene representation mapping 3D coordinates to color and density
NeRF only works with synthetic computer-generated scenes and not real-world photographs
explanation: NeRF learns a continuous volumetric representation that maps 3D coordinates and viewing directions to color and density, enabling photorealistic rendering of novel viewpoints.

text: What are the three Vs of big data?
options: Vector, Variable, Vault
Version, Vendor, Virtue
Vision, Value, Validity
Volume, Velocity, Variety
explanation: The three Vs of big data are Volume (amount), Velocity (speed), and Variety (different types), defining the core characteristics of big data.

text: What is Apache Hadoop used for?
options: Building web applications
Distributed storage and processing
Designing user interfaces
Creating mobile applications
explanation: Apache Hadoop is an open-source framework for distributed storage (HDFS) and processing (MapReduce) of large datasets across clusters.

text: What is Apache Spark?
options: A database management system
A fast distributed computing engine
A web development framework
A machine learning algorithm
explanation: Apache Spark is a fast, distributed computing engine that processes large-scale data in-memory, significantly faster than MapReduce.

text: What does HDFS stand for in big data?
options: High Data File System
Hardware Distribution Framework
Hybrid Data Format Standard
Hadoop Distributed File System
explanation: HDFS stands for Hadoop Distributed File System, designed to store very large files across multiple machines reliably.

text: What is a data lake?
options: A small SQL database
A machine learning algorithm
A centralized repository for raw data
A data visualization tool
explanation: A data lake is a centralized repository that stores raw data in its native format at any scale, supporting diverse analytics.

text: What is MapReduce in big data processing?
options: A database query language
A machine learning framework
A parallel processing programming model
A data visualization library
explanation: MapReduce is a programming model for processing large datasets in parallel across distributed clusters using map and reduce functions.

text: What is streaming data?
options: Data generated and processed continuously
Data archived for long-term storage
Data stored in static files
Data compressed for efficient transfer
explanation: Streaming data is continuously generated data that needs to be processed in real-time or near-real-time as it arrives.

text: What is a data warehouse?
options: An unstructured data lake for raw files
A tool for creating data visualizations
A physical storage building for servers
A structured repository for analyzed data
explanation: A data warehouse is a structured, organized repository optimized for analytics and reporting on historical processed data.

text: What is the purpose of data partitioning in big data systems?
options: To convert data from one format to another
To divide data across nodes for parallel processing
To encrypt data for security purposes
To delete old data from the storage system
explanation: Data partitioning divides data across multiple nodes, enabling parallel processing and improving query performance in distributed systems.

text: What is Apache Kafka primarily used for?
options: Real-time data streaming and messaging
Image processing tasks
Creating web applications
Training deep learning models
explanation: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications.

text: How does Apache Spark improve upon Hadoop MapReduce for iterative ML algorithms?
options: Spark only works with small datasets while MapReduce handles large-scale data
Spark is always slower than MapReduce but provides better fault tolerance here
Spark writes intermediate results to disk just like MapReduce for reliability
Spark keeps data in-memory across iterations avoiding costly disk read-write cycles
explanation: Spark's in-memory processing avoids the disk I/O overhead of MapReduce between iterations, making it 10-100x faster for iterative ML algorithms.

text: What is the difference between batch processing and stream processing?
options: Both approaches process data identically and the terms are completely interchangeable here
Batch processing is always faster than stream processing for all data processing workloads
Batch processes data one item at a time while streaming processes all data at once together
Batch processes all data at once while streaming processes data as it arrives continuously
explanation: Batch processing handles large accumulated datasets at once, while stream processing handles data continuously as it arrives, each suited for different use cases.

text: What is data sharding and why is it used in distributed databases?
options: It encrypts data using a shared key distributed across all machines in cluster
It horizontally partitions data across machines enabling scalability and performance
It compresses data into smaller files to reduce storage costs on each machine
It creates backup copies of all data on a single machine for redundancy purposes
explanation: Sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple machines, distributing both storage and query load for improved scalability and performance.

text: How does Apache Spark SQL enable big data analytics for users familiar with SQL?
options: It converts all SQL queries into Python code before executing them on the cluster
It replaces Apache Spark entirely with a traditional database management system
Spark SQL only works with small datasets that fit in a single machine memory
It provides a SQL interface over distributed DataFrames for familiar query syntax
explanation: Spark SQL provides a familiar SQL interface over distributed DataFrames, allowing analysts to query big data using standard SQL syntax on Spark's distributed engine.

text: What is the CAP theorem and why is it relevant to distributed data systems?
options: It states distributed systems can have consistency, availability, and partition tolerance all at once
CAP theorem only applies to single-machine databases and not to distributed systems at all
It guarantees all distributed databases will always be faster than centralized database systems
It states only two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance can be guaranteed together
explanation: The CAP theorem states distributed systems can guarantee only two of three properties: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance, forcing design tradeoffs.

text: What role does Apache Airflow play in big data engineering workflows?
options: It orchestrates and schedules complex data pipeline workflows as directed acyclic graphs
It trains machine learning models on big data using distributed gradient descent
It provides real-time stream processing capabilities for high-velocity data sources
It stores large datasets in distributed file systems across multiple cluster nodes
explanation: Apache Airflow orchestrates complex data workflows as DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs), scheduling, monitoring, and managing dependencies between pipeline tasks.

text: How does data replication improve fault tolerance in distributed systems?
options: Replication always degrades read performance to improve write speed of the system
It compresses data before storing to reduce the total storage space required
It copies data across multiple nodes so the system survives individual node failures
Replication stores data on a single machine to reduce network overhead costs
explanation: Data replication stores copies across multiple nodes, so if one node fails, data remains accessible from replicas, ensuring system availability and fault tolerance.

text: What is the difference between a data lake and a data warehouse?
options: Data lakes only support SQL queries while warehouses support all query languages used
Data lakes store raw multi-format data while warehouses store structured processed data
Data lakes store structured processed data while warehouses store raw unprocessed data
Both store data identically and the terms are completely interchangeable in practice
explanation: Data lakes store raw data in any format (schema-on-read), while warehouses store structured, processed data optimized for analytics (schema-on-write).

text: How does columnar storage format benefit analytical queries on big data?
options: Columnar storage is slower than row storage for all types of queries without exception
It stores data row by row which is optimal for reading entire records efficiently
It stores data column by column enabling efficient reads of only needed columns quickly
It only works with numerical data and cannot handle text or categorical columns well
explanation: Columnar storage (like Parquet) stores data by column, allowing analytical queries to read only the needed columns, dramatically reducing I/O and improving compression.

text: What is the role of a cluster manager like YARN in distributed computing?
options: It converts distributed queries into single-machine queries for faster processing
It allocates resources and schedules tasks across nodes in the computing cluster
It handles data encryption and security for all files in the distributed system
It manages the final output visualization of processed big data results on screen
explanation: YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) manages cluster resources, allocating CPU, memory, and scheduling application tasks across available nodes.

text: Why is exactly-once processing semantics challenging to achieve in distributed streaming systems?
options: Network failures and node crashes can cause duplicates or losses requiring complex protocols
Exactly-once processing is trivial and all modern streaming systems implement it easily
Streaming systems never experience failures so exactly-once is guaranteed automatically
Exactly-once only matters for batch processing and is irrelevant for streaming systems
explanation: Network partitions, node failures, and retries can cause messages to be processed zero or multiple times. Exactly-once requires sophisticated protocols like idempotent producers and transactional writes.

text: How does the Lambda architecture address the needs of both batch and real-time analytics?
options: Lambda architecture only supports batch processing and has no real-time component
It maintains separate batch and speed layers that serve a combined serving layer
It uses only a single processing layer for both batch and real-time data analysis
It replaces all batch processing with stream processing for unified data handling
explanation: Lambda architecture maintains a batch layer (comprehensive, accurate) and speed layer (real-time, approximate), both serving queries through a unified serving layer.

text: What is data skew and why does it degrade performance in distributed processing?
options: Data skew only affects storage and has no impact on query processing performance
Data skew always improves performance by concentrating work on the fastest machines
All distributed systems automatically handle data skew without any intervention
Uneven data distribution causes some nodes to be overloaded while others sit idle
explanation: Data skew occurs when data is unevenly distributed, causing some partitions to be much larger, overloading certain nodes while others remain idle, degrading overall performance.

text: How does the Kappa architecture simplify the Lambda architecture?
options: Kappa uses only a stream processing layer treating everything as streams eliminating batch
Kappa removes the speed layer and relies solely on batch processing for all analytics
Kappa adds a third processing layer on top of Lambda's existing batch and speed layers
Kappa and Lambda architectures are identical and the terms are completely interchangeable
explanation: Kappa simplifies Lambda by eliminating the batch layer, using only stream processing for both real-time and historical data reprocessing, reducing system complexity.

text: Why is shuffle operation one of the most expensive operations in distributed data processing?
options: Shuffle operations only move data within a single machine which is very fast operation
Shuffles only occur in batch processing and never happen in stream processing pipelines
Shuffles move data across the network between nodes causing significant IO and latency
Shuffle operations are optimized by all frameworks and never cause performance issues
explanation: Shuffles redistribute data across the network, requiring serialization, network transfer, and deserialization, creating significant I/O overhead and often becoming the bottleneck.

text: How does delta lake improve upon traditional data lake architectures?
options: It replaces the distributed file system with a single centralized database for storage
Delta lake removes all structure and stores data as completely unformatted text files
It adds ACID transactions and schema enforcement to data lake storage for reliability
Delta lake only works with structured data and cannot store any unstructured data files
explanation: Delta Lake adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel capabilities to data lakes, combining the flexibility of lakes with the reliability of warehouses.

text: What are the tradeoffs between using a wide table versus a normalized schema in big data?
options: There are no tradeoffs as both approaches produce identical query performance results
Wide tables are always superior to normalized schemas for every possible query pattern
Wide tables reduce joins but increase redundancy while normalized schemas minimize redundancy
Normalized schemas always perform better than wide tables in distributed query engines
explanation: Wide (denormalized) tables reduce expensive distributed joins but increase storage and update complexity. Normalized schemas minimize redundancy but require costly cross-node joins.

text: How does approximate query processing enable faster analytics on massive datasets?
options: It only works for count queries and cannot approximate any other aggregation types
It produces exact results faster by using more efficient algorithms on all the data present
Approximate processing always produces completely wrong results with no useful insights
It trades some accuracy for speed using sampling and sketches on subsets of the data
explanation: Approximate query processing uses techniques like sampling, sketches, and HyperLogLog to provide fast estimates with bounded error, useful when exact answers are too slow.

text: Why is data lineage tracking important in complex big data pipelines?
options: Data lineage is only useful for documentation and has no practical operational value
It traces data transformations enabling debugging, compliance, and impact analysis
Data lineage tracking always degrades pipeline performance by more than fifty percent
It only applies to batch processing and cannot track streaming data transformations
explanation: Data lineage tracks how data flows and transforms through pipelines, enabling root cause analysis, regulatory compliance, impact assessment, and data quality management.

text: How does federated learning enable ML on distributed data without centralizing it?
options: It only works with image data and cannot be applied to tabular or text data formats
Models train locally on each node and only share model updates not raw data for privacy
Federated learning always produces worse models than centralized training on all tasks
It requires all data to be collected in a central location before any training can begin
explanation: Federated learning trains models locally on distributed data, sharing only model updates (gradients) rather than raw data, preserving privacy and complying with data sovereignty regulations.

text: What does MLOps stand for?
options: Machine Learning Operations
Multi-Layer Optimization
Machine Logic Operations
Model Linear Operations
explanation: MLOps stands for Machine Learning Operations, combining ML, DevOps, and data engineering practices for reliable ML in production.

text: What is model deployment in machine learning?
options: Training a model on data
Collecting new training data
Making a trained model available for use
Deleting an old model version
explanation: Model deployment is the process of making a trained machine learning model available for real-world use, typically via APIs or embedded systems.

text: What is a REST API commonly used for in ML deployment?
options: Storing training data in databases
Serving model predictions over HTTP
Training models faster
Visualizing model architecture
explanation: REST APIs serve model predictions over HTTP, allowing applications to send data and receive predictions from deployed ML models.

text: What is model versioning?
options: Converting models between different formats
Deleting older models from storage
Tracking different versions of a trained model
Training multiple models simultaneously
explanation: Model versioning tracks different iterations of trained models, enabling comparison, rollback, and reproducibility of ML experiments.

text: What is CI/CD in the context of MLOps?
options: Calculated Input and Computed Derivation
Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment
Computer Intelligence and Cloud Delivery
Central Interface and Core Distribution
explanation: CI/CD refers to Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment, automating testing, building, and deploying ML models and code.

text: What is model monitoring in production?
options: Training the model with new data
Deleting unused model files from disk
Tracking model performance after deployment
Creating documentation for the model
explanation: Model monitoring tracks deployed model performance, data quality, and system health to detect degradation and issues in production.

text: What is a Docker container used for in MLOps?
options: Storing large datasets for training
Visualizing model predictions on charts
Packaging applications with all dependencies
Training deep learning models faster
explanation: Docker containers package ML applications with all dependencies, ensuring consistent behavior across development, testing, and production environments.

text: What is an ML pipeline?
options: A visualization of model accuracy metrics
A single model training step
A database for storing predictions
An automated sequence of ML workflow steps
explanation: An ML pipeline is an automated sequence of steps (data processing, training, evaluation, deployment) that streamlines the ML workflow.

text: What is A/B testing used for in model deployment?
options: Training two models at the same time
Splitting data into training and test sets
Merging two models into a single model
Comparing two model versions with real users
explanation: A/B testing compares two model versions by routing different user groups to each, measuring which performs better in production.

text: What is a feature store in MLOps?
options: A retail store that sells ML hardware
A centralized repository for ML features
A visualization tool for model features
A database for storing raw training data
explanation: A feature store is a centralized repository that manages, stores, and serves ML features, ensuring consistency between training and serving.

text: What is model drift and why does it require monitoring?
options: Model drift means the model is getting faster over time needing no intervention at all
Model performance degrades over time as data distributions change requiring retraining
Model drift only affects models deployed for less than one week in production systems
It refers to the physical movement of servers causing hardware failures in the system
explanation: Model drift occurs when production data distributions shift from training data, causing performance degradation that monitoring detects, triggering retraining or alerts.

text: How does canary deployment reduce risk when releasing new ML models?
options: It deploys the new model to all users simultaneously without any gradual rollout plan
It routes a small percentage of traffic to the new model while monitoring for issues
It only works for web applications and cannot be used with machine learning models
Canary deployment prevents any model from being deployed to production at all ever
explanation: Canary deployment routes a small percentage of traffic to the new model, allowing teams to monitor performance and catch issues before full rollout.

text: What is the purpose of model serialization in deployment?
options: It deletes model weights that are no longer needed after training is completed
It visualizes the model architecture as a diagram for documentation purposes only
It converts trained models into a portable format for loading in production systems
It trains multiple models in sequence one after another on the same training data
explanation: Model serialization converts trained models into portable formats (pickle, ONNX, SavedModel) that can be loaded and used in production environments.

text: How does infrastructure as code benefit MLOps workflows?
options: It eliminates the need for any cloud computing resources for ML model deployment
It requires all infrastructure to be manually configured through web interfaces only
It defines infrastructure in version-controlled code enabling reproducible environments
Infrastructure as code only applies to web development and not to ML workflows
explanation: IaC defines infrastructure in code files, enabling version control, reproducibility, and automated provisioning of consistent ML environments.

text: What is the role of experiment tracking in the ML development lifecycle?
options: Experiment tracking is optional and provides no value to the ML development process
It logs parameters, metrics, and artifacts enabling comparison and reproducibility
It only tracks the final model and ignores all intermediate experiments and results
It automatically selects the best model without any human review or intervention
explanation: Experiment tracking tools (MLflow, W&B) log hyperparameters, metrics, code, and artifacts for each experiment, enabling comparison, reproducibility, and team collaboration.

text: What is the shadow deployment pattern for ML models?
options: Shadow deployment completely ignores the old model predictions for all decisions
The new model replaces the old model immediately without any parallel operation
It deploys models only during nighttime hours when user traffic is at its lowest
The new model runs alongside the old one comparing outputs without serving users
explanation: Shadow deployment runs the new model in parallel with the production model, comparing predictions without serving the new model's results to users, reducing risk.

text: How does model compression benefit edge deployment scenarios?
options: Model compression always improves accuracy while reducing the model size significantly
It reduces model size and latency enabling deployment on resource-constrained devices
Compression eliminates the need for any preprocessing of input data at inference
It only works with linear models and cannot be applied to deep neural networks
explanation: Model compression (pruning, quantization, distillation) reduces model size and computational requirements, enabling deployment on edge devices with limited resources.

text: What is the purpose of data validation in an ML pipeline?
options: Data validation is unnecessary since all incoming data is always perfectly clean
To verify data quality and schema consistency before model training or inference
To train the model faster by removing data that takes long to process in pipeline
To convert all data types to strings for uniform processing in the ML pipeline
explanation: Data validation checks data quality, schema consistency, and statistical properties, catching issues like missing values, type mismatches, or distribution shifts early.

text: How does blue-green deployment work for ML model updates?
options: It trains the model in the blue environment and evaluates in the green one only
It gradually increases traffic to the new model over several weeks of monitoring
It maintains two identical environments and switches traffic between them instantly
Blue-green deployment only works with batch processing and not real-time serving
explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments (blue=current, green=new). Traffic switches from blue to green instantly, with easy rollback by switching back.

text: What is the benefit of using ONNX format for ML model interoperability?
options: ONNX always increases model inference time compared to native framework formats
ONNX only supports models trained with PyTorch and no other ML framework at all
ONNX provides a standard format allowing models to run across different frameworks
ONNX can only represent simple linear models and not complex neural networks
explanation: ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) provides a common format for ML models, enabling interoperability between frameworks like PyTorch, TensorFlow, and scikit-learn.

text: How does a feature store ensure consistency between training and serving in production ML?
options: Feature stores only handle training features and have no role in serving predictions
It provides a single source of truth for feature definitions and computation logic used
Feature stores always cause training-serving skew due to different code paths in them
It stores raw data only and requires separate feature computation for training and serving
explanation: Feature stores provide a centralized platform with consistent feature definitions, computation logic, and storage, ensuring identical feature values during training and production serving.

text: Why is reproducibility challenging in ML systems and how does MLOps address it?
options: ML systems are perfectly reproducible by default and require no additional tooling at all
MLOps makes all models deterministic by fixing random seeds and nothing else is needed
Reproducibility is only important in academic research and irrelevant for production ML
Non-determinism in training, data changes, and environment drift require systematic tracking
explanation: ML reproducibility is challenged by random initialization, data versioning, environment differences, and hyperparameter configurations. MLOps tools track all these systematically.

text: What is the role of a model registry in enterprise MLOps workflows?
options: Model registries are only useful for small teams and provide no value at enterprise scale
It automatically trains new models without any human oversight or approval workflows
It manages model lifecycle including versioning, staging, approval, and deployment tracking
It only stores model code and has no capability to track model metadata or lineage
explanation: A model registry serves as the central hub for managing model versions, metadata, approval workflows, lineage tracking, and deployment status across the ML lifecycle.

text: How does data-centric AI differ from model-centric AI in improving ML system performance?
options: Data-centric AI only applies to unstructured data and model-centric to structured data
Data-centric and model-centric approaches are identical and interchangeable in all cases
Data-centric focuses on improving data quality while model-centric focuses on architecture
Model-centric AI never requires any training data and relies solely on model architecture
explanation: Data-centric AI systematically improves data quality, labeling, and curation rather than focusing on model architecture changes, often yielding larger performance gains.

text: What are the challenges of implementing real-time ML inference at scale?
options: Real-time inference is trivial to implement and never poses any engineering challenges
All models inherently provide sub-millisecond inference without any optimization needed
Latency requirements, throughput scaling, and model optimization create significant complexity
Real-time inference only works with batch predictions and cannot handle individual requests
explanation: Real-time inference requires low latency, high throughput, model optimization (quantization, batching), auto-scaling, and efficient infrastructure, creating significant engineering challenges.

text: How does GitOps extend traditional DevOps practices for ML workflow management?
options: It eliminates the need for any CI/CD pipelines by manually deploying all changes to prod
It uses Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and ML pipeline config
GitOps replaces Git with a custom version control system designed only for ML artifacts
GitOps only works with data preprocessing and cannot manage model training or deployment
explanation: GitOps uses Git repositories as the single source of truth for declarative ML infrastructure and pipeline configurations, enabling auditable, automated, and reproducible deployments.

text: Why is testing ML systems more complex than testing traditional software?
options: Testing ML systems requires only standard unit tests identical to traditional software ones
ML systems are simpler to test because they only require checking if the code compiles
ML involves data dependencies, model behavior, and performance thresholds beyond unit tests
ML systems never need testing because model accuracy guarantees correct system behavior
explanation: ML testing must cover data validation, model accuracy, fairness, performance under distribution shift, integration, and system-level behavior - far beyond traditional software unit tests.

text: What is the purpose of a model serving framework like TensorFlow Serving or Triton?
options: They replace the need for any monitoring or logging of deployed model performance metrics
They optimize and serve models efficiently handling batching, versioning, and concurrency
They are only used for training models and have no capability for serving predictions
Model serving frameworks only work with a single model and cannot manage multiple models
explanation: Model serving frameworks handle efficient inference with features like dynamic batching, model versioning, multi-model serving, GPU scheduling, and health monitoring.

text: How does ML governance address regulatory requirements for AI systems in production?
options: Governance eliminates the need for any model monitoring after initial deployment is done
It only applies to models making financial decisions and not to any other domain areas
It provides audit trails, documentation, fairness monitoring, and compliance frameworks
ML governance is only relevant for academic research and has no production implications
explanation: ML governance establishes processes for model documentation, audit trails, bias/fairness monitoring, explainability, and regulatory compliance across the entire ML lifecycle.

text: What is the concept of ML technical debt and how does it accumulate in production systems?
options: Technical debt only applies to traditional software and is irrelevant for ML systems always
ML technical debt is automatically resolved by retraining the model on newer data only
ML systems never accumulate technical debt because they are self-maintaining by design
Data dependencies, pipeline complexity, and configuration debt compound over time in systems
explanation: ML technical debt accumulates through data dependencies, configuration complexity, pipeline entanglement, undeclared consumers, and monitoring gaps, often exceeding code-level debt.

text: What is AI bias?
options: A technique for improving model accuracy
A type of neural network optimization method
A method for collecting training data faster
Systematic unfairness in AI system outputs
explanation: AI bias is systematic unfairness in AI outputs, often arising from biased training data, flawed algorithms, or biased human decisions.

text: What is data privacy in the context of AI?
options: Making all data publicly available online
Sharing data freely between all companies
Protecting personal information in AI systems
Storing data without any encryption method
explanation: Data privacy in AI involves protecting personal and sensitive information from unauthorized access, use, or disclosure in AI systems.

text: What does explainable AI aim to provide?
options: Larger training datasets for models
More complex model architectures
Faster model training speeds
Understandable AI decision reasoning
explanation: Explainable AI (XAI) aims to make AI decisions transparent and understandable to humans, enabling trust and accountability.

text: What is fairness in AI systems?
options: Making AI models run on all hardware
Minimizing the size of the trained model
Ensuring equal treatment across different groups
Maximizing accuracy on the training dataset
explanation: Fairness in AI means ensuring that systems do not discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics.

text: What is the GDPR in relation to AI and data?
options: A European data protection regulation
A programming language for AI development
A type of neural network architecture
A machine learning algorithm for privacy
explanation: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is a European regulation governing data protection and privacy, significantly impacting AI systems processing personal data.

text: What is an adversarial attack on an AI system?
options: A method for training models faster now
A technique for compressing model size
Deliberately manipulating input to fool AI
Improving model accuracy by adding data
explanation: An adversarial attack deliberately manipulates input data to fool AI systems into making incorrect predictions or decisions.

text: What does transparency mean in AI ethics?
options: Making AI code run more efficiently
Reducing the size of AI model files
Hiding model details from all users
Being open about how AI systems work
explanation: Transparency in AI means being open about how AI systems work, what data they use, and how they make decisions.

text: What is informed consent in AI data collection?
options: Automatically opting in all users to data sharing
Getting user agreement before collecting data
Collecting data without user knowledge
Deleting all collected data after processing
explanation: Informed consent means obtaining clear, voluntary agreement from individuals before collecting and using their personal data for AI.

text: What is the purpose of AI regulation?
options: To prevent all AI research and development
To restrict AI use to government agencies only
To ensure AI systems are safe and responsible
To make AI technology more expensive to build
explanation: AI regulation establishes rules and standards to ensure AI systems are developed and deployed safely, fairly, and responsibly.

text: What is algorithmic transparency?
options: Making algorithms run faster on hardware
Using transparent display screens for output
Making AI decision processes understandable
Hiding algorithm details from competitors
explanation: Algorithmic transparency means making the decision-making processes of AI algorithms understandable and inspectable by stakeholders.

text: How can training data bias lead to discriminatory AI outcomes?
options: Training data bias always improves model performance and never causes discrimination
Bias in training data is automatically detected and removed by all ML algorithms used
If training data reflects historical discrimination the model learns and perpetuates it
Training data bias only affects model speed and has no impact on fairness of outputs
explanation: If training data reflects historical discrimination (e.g., biased hiring data), the model learns these patterns and perpetuates or amplifies the discrimination in predictions.

text: What is the difference between individual fairness and group fairness in AI?
options: Individual fairness focuses on groups while group fairness focuses on each individual person specifically
Both concepts are identical and always produce the same fairness outcomes for all situations
Individual fairness only applies to classification while group fairness only applies to regression
Individual fairness treats similar individuals similarly while group fairness ensures equal outcomes across groups
explanation: Individual fairness requires similar individuals to receive similar predictions, while group fairness ensures statistical parity across demographic groups.

text: What is the right to explanation under GDPR for automated decision-making?
options: It requires companies to share all source code of their AI models publicly with everyone
Individuals have the right to obtain meaningful information about the logic of automated decisions
The right to explanation only applies to decisions made by human workers not AI systems
GDPR does not address automated decision-making at all and has no such requirement
explanation: GDPR Article 22 gives individuals the right to obtain meaningful information about the logic, significance, and consequences of automated decisions affecting them.

text: How does differential privacy protect individual data in ML training?
options: Differential privacy removes all privacy protections to improve model accuracy scores
It adds calibrated noise to queries or gradients making individual data points unidentifiable
It requires all training data to be publicly available for anyone to inspect at any time
It encrypts the entire training dataset using standard encryption algorithms for security
explanation: Differential privacy adds carefully calibrated noise to computations (queries, gradients) so that the output doesn't reveal whether any individual's data was included.

text: What is the problem of automation bias in AI-assisted decision-making?
options: It refers to the bias introduced by automating the data collection process for ML
Automation bias only occurs in fully autonomous systems and never in AI-assisted ones
Users tend to over-rely on AI recommendations even when the AI may be incorrect here
Users never trust AI recommendations and always override them with their own judgment
explanation: Automation bias is the tendency of humans to favor AI recommendations over their own judgment, even when the AI is wrong, potentially leading to uncritical acceptance.

text: How can AI systems inadvertently create feedback loops that amplify bias?
options: AI feedback loops always reduce bias over time through self-correcting learning loops
Biased predictions influence future data collection which further reinforces the bias
Feedback loops only occur in recommendation systems and never in other AI applications
AI systems never create feedback loops because they process each prediction independently
explanation: When biased predictions influence actions that generate future training data, the bias gets amplified over time (e.g., predictive policing increasing arrests in over-policed areas).

text: What is the dual-use dilemma in AI technology development?
options: AI technology can only be used for beneficial purposes and never for harmful applications
Dual-use only refers to AI running on two different hardware platforms simultaneously here
AI technology developed for harmful purposes can never be repurposed for beneficial uses
The same AI technology can be used for both beneficial and harmful purposes creating tension
explanation: Dual-use refers to AI technologies that can serve both beneficial purposes (medical diagnosis) and harmful ones (surveillance, autonomous weapons), creating ethical tension.

text: What role does model interpretability play in building trust with AI stakeholders?
options: Model interpretability is only relevant for developers and has no value for end users
Interpretability reduces model accuracy so it should be avoided for all production models
All AI models are inherently interpretable and no additional effort is needed for trust
It enables stakeholders to understand and verify AI reasoning building confidence in system
explanation: Interpretability allows stakeholders to understand model reasoning, verify correctness, identify errors, and build appropriate trust, especially in high-stakes domains.

text: How does the concept of AI safety differ from AI ethics?
options: AI safety is relevant only for AGI systems while AI ethics applies only to narrow AI systems
AI safety focuses on preventing AI from causing harm while ethics addresses broader societal values
AI safety and AI ethics are identical concepts with no meaningful difference between them
AI safety only concerns physical robots while AI ethics only concerns software applications
explanation: AI safety focuses on technical measures to prevent AI from causing unintended harm, while AI ethics addresses broader questions of fairness, accountability, and societal impact.

text: What is the challenge of measuring fairness across multiple protected attributes simultaneously?
options: Intersectional groups create complexity where satisfying fairness for one may violate another
Only one protected attribute can be considered at a time and others must be fully ignored
Multiple protected attributes have no interaction and can be handled completely independently
Fairness across multiple attributes is trivial to achieve by optimizing for single attribute
explanation: Intersectional fairness is challenging because satisfying fairness criteria for one attribute (gender) may violate fairness for another (race), and combinations create more complex patterns.

text: Why is it mathematically impossible to satisfy all fairness criteria simultaneously in most real-world scenarios?
options: All fairness criteria can be satisfied simultaneously with enough training data available
Different fairness metrics are fundamentally incompatible when base rates differ across groups
Only linear models face this limitation while neural networks can satisfy all criteria easily
Impossibility only applies to theoretical scenarios and never to practical AI applications
explanation: The impossibility theorem shows that metrics like demographic parity, equalized odds, and predictive parity are mutually incompatible when base rates differ across groups.

text: How does federated learning attempt to address privacy concerns while enabling collaborative ML?
options: It trains models locally sharing only model updates while keeping raw data decentralized
It requires centralizing all data from all participants before any model training begins
Federated learning provides no privacy benefits compared to centralized training approaches
It encrypts all data using standard AES encryption and sends it to a central server only
explanation: Federated learning keeps data on local devices, sharing only model updates (gradients). However, gradients can still leak information, requiring additional privacy protections.

text: What is the value alignment problem in the context of advanced AI systems?
options: Ensuring AI systems pursue goals consistent with complex and sometimes conflicting human values
Value alignment is only relevant for recommendation systems and not for other AI applications
It refers to aligning model weights with optimal values during the training optimization
It guarantees AI systems will always act in humanity's best interest without any intervention
explanation: The value alignment problem is the challenge of ensuring AI systems pursue goals consistent with human values, which are complex, context-dependent, and sometimes contradictory.

text: How can model cards and datasheets improve responsible AI development practices?
options: They replace the need for any testing or evaluation of AI models before deployment occurs
Model cards are only useful for marketing purposes and provide no technical or ethical value
They provide structured documentation about model capabilities, limitations, and intended use
They automatically fix all bias issues in AI models without any human intervention needed
explanation: Model cards and datasheets provide standardized documentation about models and datasets, including intended use, performance across groups, limitations, and ethical considerations.

text: What is the tension between model accuracy and fairness in high-stakes AI applications?
options: Higher accuracy always guarantees better fairness outcomes for all groups simultaneously
Optimizing for fairness constraints may reduce accuracy and vice versa requiring tradeoffs
There is never any tension because accurate models are always fair to all groups by nature
Fairness constraints always improve model accuracy making the tension nonexistent overall
explanation: Fairness constraints may require accepting lower overall accuracy (e.g., equal error rates across groups may reduce aggregate accuracy), creating difficult tradeoffs in domains like healthcare.

text: How do deepfakes pose unique challenges for AI ethics and society?
options: They enable realistic fabrication of content undermining trust in media and enabling fraud
Deepfakes are easily detectable by all humans and pose no significant societal challenges
Deepfakes are only used for entertainment purposes and have no potential for misuse ever
Deepfake technology can only produce low-quality content that no one would believe is real
explanation: Deepfakes enable creation of highly realistic fake content (videos, audio) that can be used for fraud, misinformation, and harassment, eroding trust in authentic media.

text: Why is the concept of meaningful human oversight important for autonomous AI systems?
options: Meaningful oversight only applies to AI systems in military applications and no other fields
Humans must retain ability to understand, intervene, and override AI decisions when needed
Human oversight should be completely removed to maximize the efficiency of AI systems now
Autonomous AI systems never make errors so human oversight is unnecessary and wasteful
explanation: Meaningful human oversight ensures humans can understand AI decisions, intervene when needed, and maintain accountability, especially critical in high-stakes domains.

text: What are the environmental concerns associated with training large AI models?
options: Training large AI models has no environmental impact and uses negligible energy resources
Large model training consumes significant energy and computing resources increasing carbon footprint
Environmental concerns only apply to models trained on renewable energy sources specifically
Training small models produces more emissions than large models due to lower computational efficiency
explanation: Training large AI models requires massive computational resources consuming significant energy. GPT-3's training was estimated to produce hundreds of tons of CO2 emissions.

text: How does the EU AI Act categorize AI systems based on risk levels?
options: The EU AI Act bans all AI systems regardless of their risk level or application domain
The EU AI Act only applies to military AI systems and exempts all commercial applications
It treats all AI systems identically without any risk-based categorization or differentiation
It classifies systems into unacceptable, high, limited, and minimal risk with corresponding rules
explanation: The EU AI Act classifies AI into risk tiers: unacceptable (banned), high (strict requirements), limited (transparency obligations), and minimal (no restrictions), with proportionate regulation.

text: What is the challenge of ensuring AI accountability when AI systems make autonomous decisions?
options: AI systems can be held legally accountable themselves without any human responsibility needed
Accountability is only relevant when AI causes physical harm and not for any other type of impact
AI accountability is straightforward since the developer is always clearly responsible for everything
Complex AI systems make it difficult to attribute responsibility across developers, deployers, and users
explanation: Attributing responsibility for AI decisions is complex when multiple actors (developers, deployers, users) are involved, the system is opaque, and decisions emerge from complex interactions.

text: Which branch of AI focuses on enabling machines to learn from data?
options: Robotics
Machine Learning
Computer Graphics
Database Management
explanation: Machine Learning is the branch of AI focused on enabling machines to learn patterns from data without being explicitly programmed.

text: What is a robot in the context of AI?
options: A software virus
A physical or virtual agent that can perform tasks autonomously
A type of database
A programming language
explanation: A robot is a physical or virtual agent capable of performing tasks autonomously or semi-autonomously, often guided by AI algorithms.

text: Which of the following is an application of natural language processing?
options: Image compression
Voice assistants like Siri
Video rendering
Battery management
explanation: Voice assistants like Siri use natural language processing to understand and respond to human speech.

text: What does the term 'intelligent agent' mean in AI?
options: A human expert
An entity that perceives its environment and takes actions to achieve goals
A computer virus
A data table
explanation: An intelligent agent is an entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators to achieve specified goals.

text: Which of the following is NOT an AI application?
options: Spam email filtering
Self-driving cars
Manual data entry on paper
Recommendation systems
explanation: Manual data entry on paper is a purely human task that does not involve any AI technology.

text: What is computer vision in AI?
options: A way to improve monitor resolution
The field enabling computers to interpret visual information from the world
A type of encryption
A database query language
explanation: Computer vision is the AI field that enables computers to derive meaningful information from digital images, videos, and other visual inputs.

text: What type of data does a supervised learning algorithm require?
options: Only images
Labeled data with input-output pairs
Random noise
Encrypted data
explanation: Supervised learning requires labeled data where each input is paired with the correct output so the algorithm can learn the mapping.

text: What is an AI model?
options: A physical robot
A mathematical representation learned from data to make predictions
A type of hard drive
A monitor display
explanation: An AI model is a mathematical representation that has been trained on data to recognize patterns and make predictions or decisions.

text: Which industry commonly uses AI for fraud detection?
options: Agriculture
Banking and Finance
Construction
Textile manufacturing
explanation: Banking and finance commonly use AI to detect fraudulent transactions by analyzing patterns in transaction data.

text: What is the purpose of a recommendation system?
options: To delete user accounts
To suggest relevant items to users based on their preferences
To compress files
To format documents
explanation: Recommendation systems use AI to analyze user preferences and behavior to suggest relevant products, content, or services.

text: What is the difference between narrow AI and general AI?
options: Narrow AI is cheaper while general AI is expensive
Narrow AI handles specific tasks while general AI can handle any intellectual task
Narrow AI uses more data than general AI
There is no difference between them
explanation: Narrow AI is designed and trained for specific tasks, while general AI would possess the ability to perform any intellectual task that a human can do.

text: What role does a reward signal play in reinforcement learning?
options: It provides labeled examples for classification
It tells the agent how good or bad its action was
It compresses the training data
It removes outliers from data
explanation: In reinforcement learning, the reward signal provides feedback to the agent about the quality of its actions, guiding it to learn optimal behavior.

text: Which type of machine learning discovers hidden patterns in data without labels?
options: Supervised learning
Reinforcement learning
Unsupervised learning
Semi-supervised learning
explanation: Unsupervised learning algorithms discover hidden patterns and structures in unlabeled data without predefined output labels.

text: What is the role of a validation set in the machine learning workflow?
options: To train the model parameters
To tune hyperparameters and prevent overfitting during development
To collect new data from users
To deploy the model to production
explanation: The validation set is used to evaluate model performance during development, helping tune hyperparameters and detect overfitting before final testing.

text: What is semi-supervised learning?
options: A method that uses only unlabeled data
A method that combines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data
A method that only uses reinforcement signals
A method that does not use any data
explanation: Semi-supervised learning leverages both a small amount of labeled data and a larger amount of unlabeled data, reducing the need for expensive labeling.

text: What is the explore-exploit tradeoff in reinforcement learning?
options: Choosing between training and testing
Balancing trying new actions versus using known rewarding actions
Deciding between CPU and GPU usage
Selecting between classification and regression
explanation: The explore-exploit tradeoff involves balancing exploration of unknown actions to discover better strategies versus exploiting known actions that yield high rewards.

text: What is a knowledge graph in the context of AI?
options: A chart showing training loss
A structured representation of real-world entities and their relationships
A neural network architecture
A type of decision tree
explanation: A knowledge graph is a structured representation that captures entities, their properties, and the relationships between them, enabling AI systems to reason about real-world knowledge.

text: Why is data quality important for machine learning models?
options: Models always work regardless of data quality
Poor data quality leads to inaccurate or biased model predictions
Data quality only matters for image data
High quality data slows down training
explanation: Data quality directly impacts model performance. Poor quality data with errors, biases, or noise leads to inaccurate and unreliable predictions (garbage in, garbage out).

text: What is an AI pipeline?
options: A physical pipe used in factories
A sequence of data processing and modeling steps from raw data to predictions
A single algorithm that solves all problems
A hardware component in computers
explanation: An AI pipeline is a sequence of automated steps including data collection, preprocessing, model training, evaluation, and deployment that transforms raw data into predictions.

text: What is the difference between a parametric and non-parametric model?
options: Parametric models are always more accurate
Parametric models have a fixed number of parameters while non-parametric models grow with data
Non-parametric models cannot make predictions
There is no practical difference
explanation: Parametric models assume a fixed functional form with a set number of parameters, while non-parametric models make fewer assumptions and their complexity can grow with the amount of training data.

text: What is the Moravec paradox in AI research?
options: Simple computational tasks are harder for AI than complex reasoning
High-level reasoning requires less computation than sensorimotor skills
AI systems excel at tasks humans find easy but struggle with tasks humans find hard
Tasks easy for humans like perception and mobility are extremely hard for AI while abstract reasoning is comparatively easier
explanation: Moravec's paradox observes that contrary to traditional assumptions, high-level reasoning requires relatively little computation while low-level sensorimotor skills require enormous computational resources, making perception harder for AI than chess.

text: What is the difference between deductive and inductive reasoning in AI systems?
options: They are identical approaches
Deductive reasoning goes from general rules to specific conclusions while inductive reasoning infers general rules from specific observations
Deductive reasoning is always better than inductive
Inductive reasoning does not use data
explanation: Deductive reasoning applies general rules to derive specific conclusions (top-down), while inductive reasoning observes specific instances to infer general rules (bottom-up), which is the basis for most machine learning.

text: What is the computational theory of mind and how does it relate to AI?
options: Computers are conscious beings
The mind operates like a computational system processing information, providing theoretical basis for AI
All computations are intelligent
Minds cannot be modeled computationally
explanation: The computational theory of mind posits that the human mind is an information processing system and cognition is a form of computation, providing the theoretical foundation for building AI systems that simulate cognitive processes.

text: What is the Bitter Lesson identified by Rich Sutton in AI research?
options: AI research is always unsuccessful
General methods leveraging computation scale better than methods leveraging human domain knowledge
Human knowledge is always superior to machine learning
Smaller models always outperform larger ones
explanation: Rich Sutton's Bitter Lesson argues that general-purpose methods that leverage massive computation (like search and learning) ultimately outperform approaches that try to build in human domain knowledge.

text: What is the concept of emergence in complex AI systems?
options: AI systems always behave predictably
Complex behaviors and capabilities arising from simpler components that were not explicitly programmed
All AI capabilities must be manually coded
Emergence only occurs in biological systems
explanation: Emergence refers to complex behaviors and capabilities that arise from the interaction of simpler components in AI systems, where the whole exhibits properties not present in individual parts, as seen in large language models.

text: How does the concept of bounded rationality apply to AI agent design?
options: AI agents always have unlimited resources
Agents must make satisficing decisions due to limited computational resources and information
Bounded rationality means AI cannot learn
It only applies to human decision-making
explanation: Bounded rationality acknowledges that agents have limited computational resources, time, and information, so they must make satisfactory rather than optimal decisions, influencing practical AI system design.

text: What is the difference between symbolic AI and connectionist AI?
options: They are the same approach
Symbolic AI uses explicit rules and logic while connectionist AI uses neural networks to learn representations
Symbolic AI is always superior
Connectionist AI does not use mathematics
explanation: Symbolic AI represents knowledge using explicit rules, logic, and symbols, while connectionist AI (neural networks) learns distributed representations from data. Modern AI often combines both approaches.

text: What is the scaling hypothesis in modern AI research?
options: Smaller models are always better
Increasing model size, data, and compute will continue to yield improved capabilities and potentially lead to more general intelligence
Scaling always leads to diminishing returns
AI models cannot be scaled beyond a fixed size
explanation: The scaling hypothesis suggests that simply increasing model parameters, training data, and compute leads to qualitatively new capabilities, as observed in large language models exhibiting emergent abilities at sufficient scale.

text: What is the concept of an AI winter and what historically caused them?
options: A season when AI labs close for vacation
Periods of reduced funding and interest in AI due to unmet expectations and hype cycles
AI systems that only work in cold temperatures
A type of cooling system for AI hardware
explanation: AI winters are periods of significantly reduced funding, interest, and research activity in AI, historically triggered by inflated expectations followed by failure to deliver promised results, such as in the 1970s and late 1980s.

text: What is multi-task learning and why can it improve generalization?
options: Training separate models for each task independently
Training a single model on multiple related tasks simultaneously so shared representations improve performance on each task
It always reduces model accuracy
Multi-task learning only works for image tasks
explanation: Multi-task learning trains a model on multiple related tasks simultaneously, allowing the model to learn shared representations that capture common structure, acting as an inductive bias that improves generalization.

text: What is a vector in mathematics?
options: A single number
An ordered list of numbers with magnitude and direction
A type of graph
A random variable
explanation: A vector is an ordered list of numbers that represents magnitude and direction, commonly used to represent data points and features in machine learning.

text: What does the term 'dimension' refer to in a dataset?
options: The physical size of a hard drive
The number of features or variables in the data
The color depth of images
The speed of computation
explanation: In a dataset, dimension refers to the number of features or variables, where each feature adds one dimension to the data space.

text: What is the formula for calculating the mean?
options: Sum of values divided by count of values
Product of all values
Largest value minus smallest value
Square root of variance
explanation: The mean (average) is calculated by summing all values and dividing by the total count of values.

text: What is variance in statistics?
options: The middle value of a dataset
A measure of how spread out data points are from the mean
The most common value
The total count of data points
explanation: Variance measures the average squared deviation of data points from the mean, indicating how spread out the data is.

text: What is a function in mathematics?
options: A random process
A rule that assigns each input exactly one output
A type of matrix
A data storage format
explanation: A function is a mathematical rule or mapping that assigns each input value exactly one output value, fundamental to defining ML models.

text: What does the slope of a line represent?
options: The y-intercept
The rate of change between two variables
The color of the line
The length of the line
explanation: The slope of a line represents the rate of change, indicating how much the dependent variable changes for each unit change in the independent variable.

text: What is the difference between a row vector and a column vector?
options: There is no difference
A row vector is horizontal while a column vector is vertical
Row vectors are larger
Column vectors cannot be used in calculations
explanation: A row vector arranges elements horizontally in a single row, while a column vector arranges elements vertically in a single column. This distinction matters for matrix operations.

text: What is a conditional probability?
options: The probability of an impossible event
The probability of an event occurring given that another event has already occurred
A probability greater than one
The total probability of all events
explanation: Conditional probability is the probability of event A occurring given that event B has already occurred, written as P(A|B).

text: What is an exponent in mathematics?
options: A subtraction operation
The number of times a base number is multiplied by itself
A type of average
A statistical test
explanation: An exponent indicates how many times a base number is multiplied by itself. For example, 2^3 means 2 multiplied by itself 3 times, equaling 8.

text: What is the purpose of partial derivatives in machine learning?
options: To sort data alphabetically
To measure the rate of change of a function with respect to one variable while holding others constant
To create random numbers
To delete features from a dataset
explanation: Partial derivatives compute the rate of change of a function with respect to one variable while holding all others constant, essential for computing gradients in multi-variable optimization like neural network training.

text: What is a probability distribution?
options: A sorted list of numbers
A function that describes the likelihood of different outcomes for a random variable
A type of neural network
A data storage method
explanation: A probability distribution describes how probabilities are assigned to different possible values of a random variable, such as the normal distribution or Bernoulli distribution.

text: What is matrix multiplication and when is it defined?
options: Element-wise multiplication of any two matrices
Multiplying rows of the first matrix by columns of the second, defined when the first matrix's columns equal the second's rows
Always defined for any two matrices
Adding corresponding elements of two matrices
explanation: Matrix multiplication involves taking the dot product of rows from the first matrix with columns of the second. It is defined only when the number of columns in the first matrix equals the number of rows in the second.

text: What is the normal distribution and why is it important in ML?
options: A distribution where all values are equal
A bell-shaped symmetric distribution that naturally arises in many real-world phenomena due to the central limit theorem
A uniform distribution over integers
A distribution only used in physics
explanation: The normal (Gaussian) distribution is a bell-shaped, symmetric distribution important in ML because many natural phenomena follow it, and the central limit theorem ensures sample means tend toward normality.

text: What is the inverse of a matrix used for?
options: Transposing the matrix
Solving systems of linear equations and computing certain ML closed-form solutions
Increasing matrix dimensions
Sorting matrix elements
explanation: The matrix inverse is used to solve linear systems (Ax = b gives x = A^(-1)b) and compute closed-form solutions such as the normal equation in linear regression.

text: What is the central limit theorem?
options: A theorem about the center of a dataset
The sampling distribution of the mean approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of the population distribution
All distributions are normal
Larger samples are always biased
explanation: The central limit theorem states that the distribution of sample means approximates a normal distribution as sample size grows, regardless of the underlying population distribution.

text: What is the Euclidean distance between two points?
options: The Manhattan distance
The straight-line distance computed as the square root of the sum of squared differences
The cosine of the angle between them
The absolute difference of their means
explanation: Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between two points in space, calculated as sqrt(sum((x_i - y_i)^2)), commonly used in KNN and clustering algorithms.

text: What is the difference between a discrete and continuous random variable?
options: They are the same thing
A discrete variable takes countable values while a continuous variable can take any value in a range
Discrete variables are always larger
Continuous variables can only be integers
explanation: Discrete random variables take countable values (like counts), while continuous random variables can take any value within a range (like measurements), requiring different probability treatments.

text: Why is the logarithm function commonly used in machine learning?
options: It makes all values negative
It converts multiplicative relationships to additive ones and helps with numerical stability
It is only used for display purposes
Logarithms are not used in ML
explanation: Logarithms convert products to sums (simplifying calculations), compress large ranges of values, and improve numerical stability, which is why log-likelihood and log-loss are preferred over raw likelihood.

text: What is a correlation coefficient?
options: A measure of causation between variables
A value between -1 and 1 that measures the linear relationship strength between two variables
The mean of two variables
The number of data points
explanation: The correlation coefficient measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables, ranging from -1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.

text: Why is the softmax function a generalization of the sigmoid function for multiple classes?
options: They are completely unrelated functions
Softmax reduces to sigmoid for the two-class case and extends the logistic function to produce a probability distribution over K classes
Sigmoid is more general than softmax
Softmax only works with two classes
explanation: For K=2 classes, the softmax function mathematically reduces to the sigmoid function. Softmax generalizes this by normalizing the exponentials of logits across K classes to produce a valid probability distribution.

text: What is the connection between maximum likelihood estimation and cross-entropy loss?
options: They are unrelated concepts
Minimizing cross-entropy loss is mathematically equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood of the data under the model
Cross-entropy always equals zero
MLE does not apply to classification
explanation: Minimizing cross-entropy loss is mathematically equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood. Both seek model parameters that make the observed data most probable under the model's predicted distribution.

text: What is the significance of the spectral norm of a matrix in deep learning?
options: It only applies to image processing
The largest singular value that controls the Lipschitz constant and affects training stability in neural networks
It is the sum of all matrix elements
It measures matrix sparsity
